channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: What makes a school safe?
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
The stream of disturbing incidents in educational institutions refuses to let up, with violence of various kinds that suggests a deep social malaise that cuts across socio-economic categories. Those in charge of policy making and regulation respond in predictable ways, issuing orders that ramp up surveillance and punitive measures. The first takes the form of increasing securitization of schools, while the second calls for stringent action after an incident. The CBSE’s latest mandate requires all affiliated schools to install high-resolution CCTV cameras and maintain recordings for at least two weeks. These cameras are to be installed at gates, in corridors, classrooms, libraries, laboratories, playgrounds and other common areas, with washrooms exempted for reasons of privacy. In effect, there will be eyes on members of the school community almost everywhere within the premises.

There’s no doubt that surveillance cameras have helped apprehend wrongdoers and clarify the circumstances of a crime, or a troubling incident that may not count technically as a crime. There’s also an argument that the knowledge of surveillance can deter potential wrongdoers. This argument also extends to the application of severe punitive measures when crimes are committed – so the presence of the death penalty is considered a major deterrent for what a society considers “heinous crimes”. But we know only too well that the existence of stringent laws has not made things safer for vulnerable groups, often because the implementation of those laws is lax, or there are technical loopholes that allow perpetrators to go free. Moreover, the wheels of justice, as they say, move exceedingly slow, and the process can often traumatize the survivor. Surveillance systems, too, can end up being misused to impinge on privacy and freedom – would any of us want to be watched all the time, even if we’re not doing anything wrong?

So how do we make our educational spaces safer, and education itself a more nurturing experience? It is partly about having rules and reasonable security measures in place, no doubt. But a sense of safety doesn’t come only from sturdy walls and strict rules; it should also be about being safe in other, less tangible ways. Safe to speak our mind. Safe to experiment. Safe to explore and discover. Safe to make mistakes and learn from them. A school’s safety agenda should include these protections as well, for all children.

It’s a tall order, no doubt. But if schools can’t be safe spaces in all these ways, then what place can?
item.excerpt:encoded: The stream of disturbing incidents in educational institutions refuses to let up, with violence of various kinds that suggests a deep social malaise that cuts across socio-economic categories. Those in charge of policy making and regulation respond in predictable ways, issuing orders that ramp up surveillance and punitive measures.

item.title: Human skills: currency of the future
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Renata Sguario

Let’s get straight to the point: if our students can’t communicate, collaborate, or handle pressure, they won’t thrive—no matter how technically skilled they are.

We’re living through a monumental shift in what it means to be work-ready. Automation is accelerating. AI is transforming industries. And while technical knowledge still matters, it’s the human skills—resilience, self-awareness, problem-solving—that are becoming the true differentiators.

The World Economic Forum calls them the top skills for 2025. McKinsey data shows they form the bulk of foundational job readiness. And here, in India, a staggering 91% of employers now say they’re more important than hard skills. But here’s the catch: fewer than 30% of institutions teach them in any structured way.

So where does that leave us? We’re standing at a powerful intersection of possibility and responsibility.

We’ve underestimated the power of soft skills... until now

A McKinsey Global Survey (2022) shows that 43 of the 56 foundational employability skills are human-centric. And yet, most education systems remain focused on hard content and technical performance, not life readiness.

The result? A deep and growing disconnect between academic outcomes and workplace expectations.

In India, this gap is especially urgent. The Mercer | Mettl Indian Graduate Skill Index 2025 shows that only 42.6% graduates are employable—largely due to poor performance in soft skills. That’s not a pipeline issue. It’s a preparation issue.

Building work-ready humans at scale

At Maxme, we’re responding with purpose. We’ve just launched two new programs in India: ShiftU and IGNIS—tech-enabled, research-backed, and deeply human solutions designed to build capability, confidence, and career-readiness.

ShiftU is an immersive bootcamp experience for high school students. In just 2–3 days, we help young people discover their strengths, handle pressure, build confidence, and begin designing their futures.

It’s high impact, high empathy, and -- perhaps most importantly -- delivered without putting extra pressure on our already stretched educators. We bring the facilitators, we bring the tools, and we work inside the school.

IGNIS is our university program -- a hybrid model that blends digital learning on our Hodie app with real-time coaching. It’s scalable, customisable, and delivered in formats (and tailoring) that fit within academic calendars. Whether students engage for one year or three, they build critical skills that matter: leadership, collaboration, critical thinking, cultural awareness.

And yes—they graduate with a Maxme certification that signals readiness to the workforce.

We’re not replacing the curriculum -- we’re enhancing it
India’s National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 outlines a bold and holistic vision for the future of education. We’re proud that our programs align directly with its goals:

  • Multidisciplinary learning
  • 21st century skill development
  • Bridging academia and employability
  • Experiential and values-based education

Human skills are not “soft” - they’re strategic

Let’s stop calling them soft skills. They’re the hard currency of future careers. And in this new age of AI, the very skills that make us human—empathy, adaptability, creativity—are what make us most valuable.

So, what comes next?

We’re here to empower. Our work is about democratizing access to personal growth, career clarity, and confidence -- so every student, from every background, has the tools to thrive.

This is a call to action for educators, institutions, and policymakers: Let’s make human skills core business in education. Let’s build not just smarter students, but stronger humans -- ones who are creative and innovative, with empathy and self-awareness. Because, if we truly want to prepare our young people for the world ahead, it starts with what’s already inside them. We need to give them the tools to bring it out.

To bring ShiftU or IGNIS to your institution, visit www.maxme.com.au/india or email explore@maxme.com.au.

Renata Sguario had a successful corporate career for 30 years. She has now started Maxme – an organization that focuses on the critical, yet often overlooked, importance of human (soft) skills.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Renata Sguariio
Did you know that in India only 42.6% graduates are employable? It is not the subject that these students lack in, but the soft skills. It is time that our educational institutions took up training our students in empathy, adaptability, and creativity as well along with the math, science, and language.

item.title: Why interdisciplinary approach in the field of education: the story of my school
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Harshit Rana

Education is not static, it keeps evolving. According to BW Education (2022), over 80% of schools in India — especially in rural areas — still rely on traditional teaching methods, which are, more often than not, teacher-led. But, if education is evolving with time, there is a need for the teaching methods to evolve too. Schools must adopt a holistic and interdisciplinary pedagogic approach to become vibrant and thriving learning spaces.

My school, Nankamatta Public School (NPS) came into existence around 2012. In the beginning, it was just an ordinary school, dominated by traditional methods of teaching, where students were lifeless and productivity was almost dead. Until 2019, this outdated system continued. There was no space for creativity and innovation.

In 2020, when COVID-19 was all around us, a miracle happened — a turning point in the history of NPS: the introduction of the Deewar Patrika (wall magazine). The credit for this idea goes to our mentor, Mahesh Punetha.

This initiative became a spark of change. I remember the first Deewar Patrika that came out with the help of our senior students. It gave us a chance to explore the world of writing. Initially, it was a tough journey — we never thought something like this could be part of our school learning. But slowly, we realized that education is not just textbooks. Real learning happens through experience and expression.

Deewar Patrika opened the doors to many new possibilities. Though COVID-19 killed millions across the country, we were living in a rural area of Uttarakhand and somehow managed to continue our learning. We believe learning never dies. The pandemic didn't stop us — it gave us new ideas. Based on the interest and potential of students, many new things were started, one of which was community libraries . We opened around 36 community libraries across villages in Nanakmatta town.

When the lockdown ended and we went back to school, it felt like the first day again. But slowly, we adapted. With time, NPS started transforming. Now, we see NPS as a collective school, built by many invisible hands.

We started collaborating with different organizations like PARI, TFI, KER, Books For All, Cinema In School, Pratham Science Program, Involve, Bal Vigyan Khojshala, Joy of Learning Foundation, Claylab, Nature Science Initiative, and many more. Co-curricular activities became an essential part of our curriculum. Film screenings, book and science fairs, cinema, theatre, music, and activity-based learning all became a part of our lives.

We understood that learning is flexible. We can learn in our own ways. Learning can happen anywhere. We believe students can build their own paths. Many students from NPS are doing well in higher education, skill development, and collaborations.

At NPS, we believe in the power of ‘we’. And with this spirit, students are becoming changemakers. I am glad that I was a part of this process of change at NPS and hope that my school continues to inspire.

 Harshit Rana is a 12th grade student studying humanities at NPS. Belonging to a Thanu tribe family, he is a first generation student going to an English medium school. He loves writing, cooking. and calligraphy. He can be reached at hr609779@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Harshit Rana
A lot of things changed for many people after COVID-19. This school transformed from being a regular and boring school to an active and thriving learning centre.

item.title: Thank you PK for teaching me how to teach!
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Smriti Sajjanhar

Wondering how learning and teaching is connected to Rajkumar Hirani’s PK? Well, read on to find out.

It’s an eye opener, how PK, an alien, can tell us about effective learning and teaching in the pre-primary years of school.

At first glance, PK’s and a preschooler’s worlds are very different from each other’s, however if one looks deeper, striking similarities can be found. And as educators, we cannot afford to miss that.

Both are new to the world they're in

PK lands on Earth with no idea of human customs or language. Similarly, preschoolers enter the world of structured learning as complete newcomers—curious, observant, and ready to absorb everything around them.

Both ask a million questions
Both PK and preschoolers are relentlessly curious. They’re not being difficult—they’re trying to understand the world.

PK teaches us that questioning is not rebellion—it’s a path to understanding. Similarly, young children are not challenging us when they ask questions. They are trying to make sense of the world. As educators, we must nurture this questioning by creating a classroom culture where “why?” is welcomed and curiosity is celebrated.

Both learn through observation and imitation

PK copies what he sees—whether it’s folding hands in a temple or learning a language. Preschoolers do the same. They mimic behaviour, language, and emotion to make sense of life.

PK learns by doing, imitating, adapting, and trying again.
That’s exactly how preschoolers learn.

Whether it’s understanding language or relationships, PK doesn’t sit in a classroom—he lives the learning. Preschool classrooms should mirror this by offering real-life, hands-on activities that lead to deep, meaningful understanding.

Both are non-judgmental

PK doesn’t judge people—he simply sees differences. Preschoolers too are unbiased learners; they accept everyone at face value until taught otherwise. PK comes with no biases. Children come with open minds.

PK doesn’t “know” our customs; he has no preconceived ideas. He learns from observation, not instruction. Preschoolers are the same. They are sponges, learning through play, patterns, and emotional connection—not worksheets or lectures. They learn through experiences and need to be guided from where they are and not from where we expect them to be.

Both are emotionally expressive and sensitive

PK reacts intensely to kindness and confusion—he laughs, cries, and gets scared easily. Preschoolers are no different. Their emotional world is vivid and immediate. They may cry at separation, beam at praise, or shut down when ignored. Like PK, they don’t filter or hide emotions—they live them fully.

Both don’t follow rules blindly

If something doesn’t make sense, PK challenges it. PK doesn’t just accept rituals—he questions them. Preschoolers too often question “rules” because they haven’t internalized societal norms yet—and that’s a strength not a flaw. They often defy norms not out of mischief but because they haven’t yet absorbed social scripts. They are trying to find logic and meaning. Preschoolers are natural explorers—constantly questioning, experimenting, and navigating the unknown.

Applying the PK perspective: interconnected learning

To apply these ideas into practice, an interconnected learning module can be deployed. The three pillars of interconnected learning must be in sync to achieve desired goals.

Learning area – the skill or developmental domain
Classroom activity – the experiential, play-based task
Reinforcement tool – books or structured resources to anchor the concept

Just as PK’s understanding evolved through real-life interactions, emotional engagement, and reflective thought, so too can preschoolers thrive when these three key elements work in harmony.

Rethinking the role of the educator

Just like the characters in PK who had to drop their beliefs to truly understand PK, educators too must be willing to unlearn rigid practices. Teaching in early years is less about delivering content and more about:

  • Creating safe, emotionally rich environments.
  • Supporting multiple domains of development.
  • Letting children explore, err, and grow through guided discovery.

PK taught us that truth reveals itself when we’re willing to see differently. So do children. When we stop enforcing and start engaging, stop correcting and start connecting, we give preschoolers the space to learn the way they were born to: through curiosity, compassion, and play.

So, the next time a child asks, “Why is the moon following me?”—don’t rush to explain.
Pause. Wonder with them.
Because in that moment, learning has already begun.

PK as a teacher training tool

PK is more than just a film—it’s a philosophy. It compels us to look at curiosity, empathy, and openness when we are learning. It holds up a mirror to our old, rigid systems and gently shows the contemporary way.

If every educator watched PK not just as a movie, but as a training tool, we’d witness a shift in the way we teach. Shift from instruction to inspiration. From correction to connection.

Because PK teaches us what no manual can—that learning begins with listening, thrives on love, and blossoms in freedom.

Let’s officially include PK in every teacher training curriculum—not for its cinematic brilliance, but for its timeless lessons in humanity, inquiry, and learning with heart.

Thank you, PK, for reminding us how to teach. And more importantly, how to learn.

Smriti Sjjanhar has over 30 years of experience in education. She specializes in play-based learning, differentiated instruction, and teacher development. Her learner-centric approach blends creativity with professionalism to create joyful, meaningful experiences that nurture curiosity, critical thinking, and life-ready skills in students. She can be reached at smritis@genesisgs.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Smriti Sajjanhar
We have often used films or film clips to excite children about concepts we are teaching, but as teachers have we ever wondered whether we can learn something from films too?

item.title: About to yell ‘wake up!’ at your teen? Pause. Read this
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Nikhil Pant

Your teenager just woke up at 11 AM… again, while half your day has already passed. As you enter their room, you find them yawning and contemplating their decision to wake up or not…. As they are about to smash their head on the pillow, you shout, “Get up and stop being lazy!”

But here’s the thing — they aren’t being lazy. They are in their repair mode. The teenage brain is the most exciting, enticing, and confusing subject to study and talk about. With so many changes — physical, emotional, and social — the brain becomes the centre of all the chaos.

No, I’m not trying to defend teenagers waking up at 11 AM and get their brownie points just so they attend my classes and stop bullying me. I’m simply stating what brain science has been showing.

Teenage sleep patterns aren’t just a discipline issue; they’re actually driven by cognitive variations — something entirely misunderstood by adults.

So, let’s try to understand what exactly happens (the science bit):

At the onset of puberty, the teenage body welcomes a number of new hormones — especially testosterone in males and estrogen in females — also called sex hormones. As much as the parents would like to deny it, the truth remains: yes, these hormones exist in your teenager’s body, just like they did in yours a couple of decades ago (nothing personal).

The sex hormones impact the circadian rhythm — the internal body clock — by delaying the production of melatonin (the sleep hormone) in the teenage body. These hormones interact with the pineal gland, which controls melatonin secretion, and delay the process, resulting in a 1–2 hour delay in melatonin production compared to children and adults.

So, no matter how many times you switch off their lights or take rounds of their room to check if they’ve slept, until their body starts producing melatonin, they simply won’t be able to fall asleep.

So, are we blaming everything on the sex hormones? Absolutely not! This is where most parents will be happy — yes, the phones, laptops, and other electronic devices are equally responsible.

All electronic devices emit blue light, which the teenage brain is extremely sensitive to. As a result, the brain starts thinking it’s still daytime, further suppressing melatonin production — leading to even later nights.

Due to these late nights, teenagers aren’t able to get the recommended 8–10 hours of sleep suggested by the National Sleep Foundation for adequate cognitive development. Anything less than that may impact the prefrontal cortex, which is responsible for basic functions like judgment, emotional regulation, impulse control, and long-term planning. Sleep deprivation can lead to:

Higher irritability and emotional reactivity

Poor academic focus

Weaker immune response

Increased risk of risky behaviour

All thanks to a tired prefrontal cortex.

No matter what time teenagers go to bed, they still have to get up by 7 AM to catch the first period. Do the math and answer for yourself: Is your child getting 8 hours of sleep? If not… stop blaming them for being moody, scoring fewer marks, or feeling lazy all the time.

They are in a state of sleep deprivation — all starting with the delayed release of melatonin, caused by a shift in the biological clock, hormonal changes, and modern lifestyle habits.

Nikhil Pant has so far had five fantastic years of experience teaching innovative subjects like global perspective, thinking skills, and wellbeing across diverse national and international boards. He can be reached at nikhilpant2505@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nikhil Pant
The cell phone, ipad, and laptop may not be the only causes for your teenager’s late nights.

item.title: Assessing change: trends and transitions in school assessment in India
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Simran Luthra

Let me begin with a disclaimer. This article is an ambitious attempt. It does not claim to be comprehensive or definitive. Instead, it aims at being a conversation starter by provoking thought and reflection. It is an attempt to reflect on and discuss the current trends and status of a critical aspect of school education: assessment – a factor that determines the worth and quality of teaching and learning, as well as institutional status. However, given where we stand today, I argue that assessment is a complex, variegated, and uneven phenomenon in India. Hence, rather than offering definitive answers, this article aims to explore current trends, unpack ideas, and make suggestions.

Alright, now that I feel satisfied after that elaborate disclaimer, let us outline some facts about assessment policy reform in India to set the context:

  • Policy reform in assessments was first attempted in the National Curriculum Framework (NCF) 2005, which sought to shift pedagogy to a constructivist mode from a rote-based one. NCF 2005 also introduced Continuous and Comprehensive Evaluation (CCE) with the aim of reducing pressure on students and for ‘diagnosis, remediation and enhancing of learning’ (NCF 2005).
  • The Right to Education Act (2009) further emphasized CCE and introduced the no-detention policy.
  • The National Education Policy 2020 and NCF 2023 emphasize experiential learning, competency-based learning outcomes, and continue to reiterate and emphasize CCE focusing on learning outcomes over rote learning. There is a renewed emphasis on assessment for, of, and as learning.
  • In December 2024, the no-detention policy for grades 5-8 was discontinued amid concerns that students were being promoted without achieving the required learning outcomes. The policy change also mandates remedial instruction and re-examination opportunities.

Policy on paper and policy on ground are two very different realities. I, therefore, spoke to teachers – those who are expected to be the agents of policy reform – to get a sense of their experiences and understanding of where assessments stand at present.

Scholars consistently assert how teachers’ voices are crucial to understanding ground realities. At the same time, in the Indian research context, teachers’ voices remain marginalized (Batra, 2005). Although my approach is journalistic rather than that of rigorous academic research, I believe it is essential to pay attention to what teachers are saying as their insights can both indicate gaps as well as possible solutions.

I spoke to a mix of government school and private school teachers. What emerged was a picture of differential experiences of teaching and assessing. There were region-wise differences, with Delhi teachers reporting high adherence to the latest NEP and NCF recommendations and lower adherence in states such as Kerala, Telangana, and Bihar.

Further, differences in teacher experiences stemmed from two key factors: school type and socio-economic profile. Government schools, with stricter compliance requirements, saw teachers adhering more closely to activity-based and experiential methods due to documentation expectations. Additionally, teachers in resource-rich private schools had vastly different experiences compared to those in low-fee private schools, highlighting the impact of socio-economic disparities. Thus, teachers are able to experiment with constructivist pedagogy, which is activity/project-based with greater ease with students who have support at home and/or internet access.

In what follows, I have summarized insights that emerged from their interviews into themes and/or trends. If you are a teacher or educator, it could be that some of these resonate with your experiences while others do not.

Pressure of documentation

Most teachers shared how the shift in focus from summative to formative assessments has resulted in extensive documentation for them. This includes both paper-pen documentation as well as digital, meaning double the labour. This is a known problem, but one that remains unsolved.

The introduction of the Holistic Progress Card (HPC) as recommended by NEP 2020 and NCF 2023 is not uniform with some schools having implemented it independently and others not. Further, the form in which the HPC is used is also not uniform, as there is no set template for it yet and schools and service-providers have interpreted it differently. However, in instances where it has been introduced, based on the suggested format, teachers have found certain sections to be redundant or overkill.

Divya,[*] for instance, shared how she considered it a waste of time for children to evaluate their peers on academics, which is a field that is part of their HPC document. On the other hand, Sherin reported how the lack of clear guidelines on how to carry out peer evaluation made it hard for teachers to implement it even though she believes it is something that can and should be done. Both Divya and Sherin teach in government schools in Delhi and Kerala respectively.

Sherin also shared how the pressure to enter scores or grades for multiple formative assessments mandatorily and frequently often encouraged teachers to enter marks at random, thus defeating the very purpose of assessing and reducing it to a mere formality rather than a meaningful activity.

Alignment of pedagogy and assessments

I was particularly interested in exploring the alignment between pedagogy and assessments. India has had a strong textbook-centric school culture, and the teachers’ own socialization has taken place in the rote and textbook-oriented system, something that Shraboni, a private school teacher from Ranchi, also acknowledged. Shifting from this pre-existing textbook culture to a child-centric one that promotes ‘joyful learning’ and is activity and project-based has been long coming.

From a policy perspective, as early as 1966, the Kothari Commission Report and then the New Policy on Education (NPE), 1986, both recommended child-centric approaches. NPE 1986, in fact, first introduced the adjective ‘joyful’ for school education in India: By making elementary education child-centred, we would be introducing a long-awaited reform in the system. The most important aspect of this reform will be to make education a joyful, inventive and satisfying learning activity, rather than a system of rote and cheerless, authoritarian instruction. (NPE, 1986).

However, even after four decades, textbooks remain firmly entrenched in the Indian classroom and policy documents continue to make similar recommendations.

It would, however, be wrong to say that nothing has moved. Most teachers reported a pedagogical shift from a textbook-oriented one to activity-based methods. Driven by policy and supported by training initiatives, activity-based learning is becoming the norm in most schools. This is also supported by teachers’ own reflection on the kind of rote-based education they experienced and wanting to provide a more meaningful experience to their students. Thus, pedagogical reform seems to be evident. But, what about assessment reform?

Shifts in assessment seem to be much more uneven despite policy recommendations. Various studies have shown how CCE, despite being a progressive move, did not meet much success (Juneja, 2018; Berry et al, 2020) after its introduction in NCF 2005. The failure is attributed to various reasons, ranging from conceptual differences in policy and implementation frameworks that have created confusion from the outset (Yagnamurthy, 2015), the lack of orientation of teachers towards policy changes in assessments (Brown et al, 2014), and other systemic factors such as classroom size, focus on syllabus completion, and so on that make implementation realistically difficult. The teachers’ narratives that inform this article corroborate most of the existing research, which is, in fact, minimal and scant.

Divya and Shraboni, both lamented that in their respective schools, teachers focused on joyful and activity-based learning throughout the year, but that the rote-based pen-and-paper exams were not in alignment with such a pedagogical approach. Sherin elaborated on this further when she said that at times children who are not the best at expressing themselves through writing but have shown evidence of conceptual understanding through activities, get ‘poor results’. The reverse is more common: children who are able to rote learn well, end up with good marks despite lacking conceptual understanding. In such cases, the teachers strongly feel that there is a misalignment between pedagogy and assessments.

‘Formative’ assessment: now a misnomer?

The purpose of formative assessment is assessment for learning, as an input for teachers to adjust instruction or address conceptual or skill gaps. By definition, it is supposed to be low stakes, ongoing, and flexible. However, most teachers reported that ‘FAs’ are conducted as paper-pen tests of lesser marks (typically 20 or 30) and function as low stakes exams. Instances of formative assessment not being paper-pen based are rare but not nil.

Malati, whose teaching career spans three decades, shared how CCE in the 2010s was substantially different from its current form. Formative assessment consisted of projects or hands-on activities and was true to its definition and purpose. She highlighted how the sudden shift then became overwhelming for teachers and students alike, with all subject teachers assigning multiple projects throughout the year, leading ultimately to rejection of the CCE model nearly a decade ago. However, now the ‘FA’ component is equal to what used to be called ‘unit tests’ earlier; almost defeating the purpose of it being ‘formative’ in nature. In Malati’s school, which is a private school in Delhi, the teachers and management have struck a middle-ground where they have gone back to implementing CCE in its original spirit but ensuring they do not overburden students or teachers with too many projects.

English teachers shared that while their classroom activities focused on speaking exercises along with reading and writing, a holistic approach that catered to the four skills of Listening, Speaking, Reading, and Writing (LSRW) was sorely missing in English assessments at all levels from primary to high school with overemphasis on reading and writing. Only in very limited instances did teachers report that formative assessments were built around debates or speaking tasks. This is a major concern because it means that language continues to be taught as a ‘subject’ as opposed to as a set of competencies.

Are higher-order or critical thinking skills being tested?

When asked if the question papers (summative and/or formative) contained questions pertaining to critical or higher-order thinking skills, most teachers said they did not. Only a few said that one or two such questions were included, but overall the papers were rote-based.

Further, it is mostly in private schools with children from resource-rich families, where higher-order or critical thinking skills were a focus in assessments. Students in schools belonging to the opposite end of the spectrum struggle owing to low reading levels.

The role of foundational literacy in assessments

According to teachers, a major impediment when answering pen-and-paper exams was language proficiency. Low reading and writing levels seem to be responsible for unsatisfactory exam performance. Foundational literacy (along with numeracy) has been rightly identified as a gap by NEP 2020 and NCF 2023, which strongly recommend the development of these basic skills, particularly by grade 3, so that students can become independent learners at the earliest. However, the actual realization of this is not as straightforward owing to language being taught more as a ‘subject’ than as a set of skills as mentioned earlier.

Parents and assessments

While pedagogy and curriculum are aspects that parents in mainstream Indian schools hardly get into, when it comes to assessments that is not the case. Parent-Teacher Meetings are typically ‘report-card days’, where results from formative and summative assessments are shared with parents and hence become high stakes.

Some teachers reported that the recent shift from marks to grades was something that several parents were not used to and how they still demand percentages and ranks, as problematic as these measures are. This indicates how culturally embedded schooling practices become and how it is difficult to pivot from them.

At the same time, sudden policy changes can cause confusion. The scrapping of the no-detention policy is a case in point. Divya noted that students who have never faced detention due to exam performance are now being caught off guard. As a result, teachers are having to counsel both parents and students through this transition.

On a more hopeful note, a positive generational shift, reported by Divya, was that younger parents seem to be less concerned with marks and grades. A significant chunk of parents of younger children are millennials who are more keen on holistic development and eager to protect their children’s mental health from the tyranny of marks and grades.

Some key lessons…

Here are some vital learnings based on the teachers’ narratives.

Everyone can do something in their context: Teachers continue to innovate despite constraints. Priya, a headmistress and teacher from a school in Hyderabad, shared that students in her school have limited internet access and minimal academic support at home. To adapt, teachers design project-based formative assessments that require extra effort but are necessary given the students’ socio-economic backgrounds. Efforts are made to ensure that students relate concepts with their daily lives: in maths, students create word problems based on daily life, while social studies projects focus on current affairs, with teachers providing additional support. Such approaches reflect teachers’ dedication to making learning relevant and engaging despite challenges.

NEP/NCF options to be available across states: NCF 2023 allows students to drop traditional subjects and take up vocational subjects such as music, information technology, or entrepreneurship from grade 9 onwards. Malati shared how this was being implemented in her school for the last couple of years and has been received very well by students.

It was interesting to hear Priya raise this same concern. She shared how academics was not every student’s cup of tea and that vocational options should be available to students. Hence, while vocational options are available to students in Delhi, they are not available to students across the country, particularly in state-board schools. This is something to be considered from a policy perspective.

Classroom size to be contained: Most teachers shared how their class size was 40 on average. Malati said that in her career of nearly three decades, she had witnessed a steady rise in the number of students per class. However, as she mentioned, this has an adverse effect on the connection a teacher is required to make with students and the individual attention each child needs. Meaningful and regular formative assessments also get compromised if the class size is too large. School leaders and management need to address this issue if they aim to provide quality education.

The bane of ‘syllabus completion’: One of the remnants of a textbook-centric culture is the expectation that the entire book must be ‘covered’ by the teacher from end to end. However, when ‘syllabus coverage’ takes priority over depth and conceptual clarity, it becomes counterproductive. Further, if assessments remain rote-oriented, it puts even greater pressure on students. Schools and teachers should realistically and critically rationalize the syllabus and greater autonomy should be given to teachers to make such decisions.

Leveraging technology to make teachers’ lives easier: Authorities at both state and school levels should take steps to eliminate redundant documentation for teachers. Timely feedback shared by teachers on the items to be documented can help reduce redundancies or misaligned fields to be filled which can both save time and effort and also avoid erroneous or low-quality data. Again for this, we need to listen to teachers.

To conclude…

As I conclude, all that remains unsaid weighs heavy on me. There is so much more that the teachers shared that remain outside of my word count limitations for this article. And I would reiterate here that the intention of this article is to get us thinking more and more critically about assessment practices.

I originally set out to write this article to explore the complex relationship between pedagogy and assessments. We know from anecdotal records and considerable research that assessments impact pedagogy; the ‘teach to test’ phenomenon, for instance, in which teachers tend to teach in a manner that aligns with how students will fare in exams at the expense of deeper learning or broader curricular goals (Phelps, 2011).

There is ample research evidence that summative assessments affect the pedagogic practices of teachers (Erskine, 2014). With standardized testing becoming the global norm, researchers have found increasingly, a narrowing of curricula taking place, with a growing focus on testable content where memorization takes priority over understanding to achieve high scores. It has also been found that this leads to examination subjects gaining a higher status than non-examinable subjects such as physical education, art and craft, and music. The teacher narratives echo the patterns observed in these wider studies. Assessments hence deserve the sort of focus and effort dedicated to pedagogy.

It is important to clarify that I entered the ‘field’, so to speak, with my own hypothesis or biases. I thought most teachers I would speak with would be despondent or that there would be considerable confusion. Teachers, however, are an optimistic lot and a wealth of ideas, perspectives, and energy reside in them. Greater collaboration and knowledge sharing among educators, coupled with consistent dialogue and rigorous research, are essential for working towards meaningful and effective assessment practices and policies.

References

  • Batra, P. (2005). Voice and Agency of Teachers: Missing Link in National Curriculum Framework 2005. Economic and Political Weekly, 40(40), 4347-4356. http://www.jstor.org/stable/4417232
  • Berry, J., Kannan, H., Mukherji, S., & Shotland, M. (2020). Failure of frequent assessment: An evaluation of India’s continuous and comprehensive evaluation program. Journal of Development Economics, 143, 102406.
  • Brown, G. T. L., Chaudhry, H., & Dhamija, R. (2015). The impact of an assessment policy upon teachers' self-reported assessment beliefs and practices: A quasi-experimental study of Indian teachers in private schools. International Journal of Educational Research, 71, 50-64. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijer.2015.03.001
  • Erskine, J. L. (2014). It changes how teachers teach: How testing is corrupting our classrooms and student learning. Multicultural Education, 21(2), 38-40.
  • Juneja, N. (2018). Policy lessons for inclusion from the fate of the CCE within the performativity culture in education. Vikalpa, 43(1), 14-23.
  • Phelps, R. P. (2011). Teach to the Test? The Wilson Quarterly (1976), 35(4), 38-42.
  • Yagnamurthy, S. (2017). Continuous and comprehensive evaluation (CCE): policy and practice at the national level. The Curriculum Journal, 28(3), 421-441.

[*] Names changed to maintain confidentiality of teacher identities.

Simran Luthra holds a PhD in Education from the School of Education, Tata Institute of Social Sciences (TISS), Mumbai. She also has a Master’s in English from Jadavpur University, Kolkata, and a Master’s in Education from TISS, Mumbai. A former Fulbright Foreign Language Teaching Assistant at Stanford University (2013-14), Simran has over a decade of experience in the field of education. Currently based in Pune, she continues to contribute to education through her research, teaching, and writing. She can be reached at simranluthra@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Simran Luthra
As education policy evolves, Simran Luthra writes about what assessments actually look like on the ground.

item.title: Will the new CBSE two-exam model for class X benefit all of India?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
S Mundayoor

CBSE has finally confirmed a major change in the class X examination schedule from 2026. The final board exams will be advanced from March, when they are usually held, to February. There will be an optional second exam in May for those who would like to try and better their scores. CBSE had, in the first week of March 2025 sought feedback on this proposal from schools, state governments and individuals, but it appears that there was hardly any discussion on the impact of this proposal in the media or other public forums.

On the face of it, this proposal looks very attractive and seems compact: students are being provided with one more chance to improve their performance, if their first attempt was not satisfactory. The first exam is compulsory for all. Secondly, the proposal aims to complete all CBSE exams in less than a month, from Feb 15 to March 6. The results for class X will be ‘available’ on April 20 in DigiLocker, but will not be formally ‘announced’ till June 30. Thirdly, the second examination is optional for students and is proposed to be held from May 5 to May 20. CBSE will declare the formal results on June 30. Hence, class XI “admissions and session can commence” from the last week of April itself.

But the question is: Do CBSE schools in all states in India begin their new academic session in April? In Arunachal Pradesh, for instance, where only the CBSE Board exists, all schools – both private and government – open only by June every year. So do the CBSE schools in Kerala, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu. Even if it were by an order of the Union Ministry of Education, would it be fair to ask the schools that have just started their academic sessions by mid June 2025 to curtail their class X sessions and get ready for the final exams by February 2026? Can one cot fit all?

For decades, the CBSE class X and XII exams were being held from March to mid April. This meant that school sessions and activities in January and February were practically undisturbed by exam schedules. Nor did the CBSE or school-level exams clash with the festivals of Arunachal Pradesh, most of which fall in February. Now, according to this new timetable, a major exam for class XII will have to be written on February 20 – Arunachal Pradesh’s Statehood Day!

These were a few disturbing thoughts that emerged when CBSE made this proposal in March 2025. Conducting two exams, one after another in a span of two months: will this benefit or is it suitable for students in Arunachal Pradesh, when 62.6% of them are in government schools (ASER Report 2023), and most of them rural students, staying and studying in schools far away from their homes?

In an article in the Arunachal Times, March 6, 2025, (https://tinyurl.com/23z92k3n), I had raised these issues. A submission was also made to the CBSE Chairman on March 9, 2025, explaining the unsuitability of a sudden change in CBSE exam schedules for the government school students of Arunachal Pradesh. I also pointed out that –

1. For most government school students in Arunachal Pradesh, passing the class X exam itself posed several challenges (in 2023-24, the class X results in Arunachal Pradesh was 49%, this year, 56.1%);

2. The proposed second exam in May falls in the agricultural season in Arunachal, when most students from the rural communities are involved in family farming activities. For families from other states serving in Arunachal, May is the only time they get to go to their hometowns.

3. May being a rainy month, Arunachal Pradesh suffers from frequent disruptions in power and internet connectivity, making it difficult to learn and read. The students will also have no mentors to guide them to prepare for the second exam.

4. Since most students study in schools located far away from their villages, staying in these school-towns in May will cause the families, especially of adolescent girls, accommodation and financial burden.

5. Teachers deployed for examination duties in May will go on their vacations between June and August, after schools re-open. This will lead to further academic instability in government schools, where skilled subject teachers are always in shortage.

Hence, the second exam proposed by the CBSE may be of interest only to a section of students in the few Kendriya Vidyalayas (KV) and Jawahar Navodaya Vidyalayas (JNV) (which follow a common academic calendar across India, no matter which state they are in) and the expensive private schools in Arunachal.

Before the new exam timetable is introduced, Arunachal Pradesh will have to re-schedule its academic calendar to begin in April. Such an overnight change is neither feasible nor suitable.

Hence, it will be in the best interest of the student-community, if the CBSE –

i)     Initially implements the two-exam scheme on a pilot basis in the metros, KVs, and JNVs, before rolling it out across the country.

ii)    Introduces a flexible exam schedule, taking into consideration the local socio-cultural and climatic conditions of different regions in the country. While some states may find February convenient, states like Arunachal will prefer March. Even if a change is desirable, it should give the states adequate time to adjust to the new proposal.

Shouldn’t our school academic and examination calendar be planned to suit the climatic and socio-cultural background of each state? Something for our education planners and leaders to think about?

S Mundayoor is an educational and reading promotion activist in Arunachal Pradesh, with over 44 years of experience with Arunachali youth. He is the coordinator of the Lohit Youth Library Network. He can be reached at lohit.libraries@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
S Mundayoor
CBSE’s new plan may help some, but hurt others. S Mundayoor poses some questions about what this means for students in different parts of the country.

item.title: Assessment and evaluation: what they mean in the math classroom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

In our mission as educators, particularly in the realm of mathematics, we constantly strive to understand how well our students are grasping the intricate world of numbers, shapes, and patterns. Two terms that frequently surface in our discussions about student progress are "assessment" and "evaluation." While often used interchangeably, it's crucial for us, as teachers, to understand their distinct roles and how both can be leveraged to create more impactful learning experiences in our middle school math classrooms.

Think of assessment as a continuous journey of gathering information about student learning during the instructional process. It's like a doctor regularly checking a patient's vital signs to monitor their health and make necessary adjustments to the treatment plan ("Assessment and Evaluation", n.d.). In our classrooms, assessment involves employing various techniques to understand what our students know, what they can do, and where they might be facing challenges. This feedback loop is invaluable for both us, as teachers, to refine our teaching methodologies, and for our students, to gain clarity about their learning journey.

Evaluation, on the other hand, is akin to a final diagnosis. It involves using the data gathered through assessments to make judgments about student learning at a specific point in time, often culminating in grades or reports ("Assessment and Evaluation", n.d., p. 1). While evaluation plays a vital role in the academic structure, particularly in determining progress to the next level, its focus is primarily on summarizing learning rather than actively guiding its development (Knowly, 2020).

Summative evaluation

Reflecting on our own experiences as students in India, many of us might recall a system heavily reliant on summative evaluations – the unit tests and final examinations that primarily aimed to assign grades and determine promotion ("Rethinking classroom assessment", 2006). While these evaluations provided a snapshot of our performance at a particular moment, they often lacked the immediate, actionable feedback that could have truly propelled our learning forward, especially in subjects where we struggled. The emphasis was often comparative, sometimes even leading to public comparisons that could be disheartening and fail to address individual learning needs (Next School, 2016).

The modern educational landscape calls for a more balanced approach, one that recognizes the power of both assessment for learning (formative assessment) and assessment of learning (summative assessment).

The power of formative assessment: guiding the learning journey

Formative assessment is like the compass and map for our students' learning journeys. It provides ongoing feedback that helps them understand where they are, where they need to go, and how to get there. It's about embedding assessment activities seamlessly into our daily lessons to gain real-time insights into student understanding.

Here are some practical examples of formative assessment we can readily incorporate into our middle school math classrooms:

  • Quick quizzes and polls: Short, ungraded quizzes or interactive polls (using online tools or even simple hand signals) at the beginning or during a lesson can quickly gauge students' prior knowledge or immediate comprehension of a concept.
    • Example (Integers): Before introducing the addition of negative integers, a quick poll asking students to define "negative numbers" or solve simple addition problems with positive integers can reveal their foundational understanding.
  • Think-pair-share: Posing a question or problem, allowing students the time to think, discuss their ideas with their partner, and then share their thoughts with the whole class. This encourages active participation and reveals different approaches to problem-solving.
    • Example (Fractions): After explaining the concept of equivalent fractions, ask students to think of an equivalent fraction for 52​, pair them with a classmate to discuss their methods, and then share a few of the generated equivalent fractions with the class.
  • Exit tickets: Short prompts or questions given to students at the end of a lesson to assess their key takeaways or identify any lingering confusion.
    • Example (Algebraic expressions): An exit ticket asking students to write down one thing they learned about combining like-terms and one question they still have can provide valuable feedback on the effectiveness of the lesson.
  • Observation and questioning: Carefully observing students as they work on tasks and asking targeted questions to understand their reasoning and problem-solving strategies.
    • Example (Geometry): While students are constructing triangles with specific angle measurements, observe their techniques and ask questions like, "Why did you choose that angle?", or "How do you know these two sides will meet?"
  • Self and peer assessment: Providing students with rubrics or clear criteria to evaluate their own work or the work of their peers. This fosters metacognition and a deeper understanding of learning objectives.
    • Example (Data handling): After a project on representing data using bar graphs, provide students with a rubric to self-assess their graph based on elements like correct labelling, accurate representation of data, and clear title. They can then use the same rubric to provide constructive feedback to a classmate.
  • Two stars and a wish: A simple feedback strategy where students provide two positive comments (stars) about a piece of work and one suggestion for improvement (wish).
    • Example (Mensuration): After solving problems on areas of rectangles, students can review a classmate's solution and offer two things they did well and one area where they could be more precise, perhaps in their calculations or explanations.

Formative assessment is not about assigning marks; it's about providing timely and specific feedback that empowers students to take ownership of their learning and make necessary adjustments (Knowly, 2020). It helps us teachers to identify learning gaps early on and adapt our instruction to address those needs effectively ("Rethinking classroom assessment with purpose in mind", 2006).

The role of summative evaluation: measuring overall achievement

Summative evaluation, while focused on assessing learning at a specific point, still holds significance in our educational system. It helps us gauge the overall achievement of learning objectives and provides data for reporting student progress. However, it's crucial that summative assessments are designed in a way that truly reflects student understanding and not just rote memorization.

Examples of summative evaluation in middle school math include:

  • Unit tests: Covering specific topics or units of study. These should ideally include a variety of question types (multiple choice, short answer, problem-solving) to assess different levels of understanding.
    • Example (Ratio and proportion): A unit test on ratio and proportion might include questions on finding equivalent ratios, solving direct and inverse proportion problems, and applying these concepts to real-life scenarios like sharing amounts in a given ratio.
  • Mid-term and annual examinations: Comprehensive assessments covering a broader range of topics. These should aim to assess not just factual recall, but also the ability to apply concepts and solve more complex problems.
    • Example (Algebra): A midterm exam on algebra might include questions on simplifying expressions, solving linear equations and inequalities, and applying algebraic concepts to word problems.
  • Projects and presentations: Allowing students to demonstrate their understanding through in-depth exploration of a topic and presenting their findings. This can assess not only mathematical knowledge but also research, communication, and critical thinking skills.
    • Example (Geometry): A project where students research different types of quadrilaterals, their properties, and real-world applications, culminating in a presentation with visual aids and explanations.
  • Performance tasks: Engaging students in activities that require them to apply their mathematical knowledge and skills to solve realistic problems.
    • Example (Data handling): A performance task where students collect data on a topic of interest (e.g., favourite sports of classmates), organize it in a table, create appropriate graphs, and interpret the findings.

To make summative evaluations more equitable and reflective of true understanding, we can:

  • Provide clear rubrics: Sharing the criteria for assessment beforehand helps students understand expectations and focus their learning accordingly.
  • Offer choices: Where appropriate, allowing students to choose from different assessment tasks can cater to their strengths and interests.
  • Incorporate real-world applications: Designing problems that connect mathematical concepts to real-life situations can make the assessment more meaningful and relevant.

Striving for a balanced approach

The key to effective assessment and evaluation in middle school math classrooms lies in achieving a harmonious balance between formative and summative approaches. By weaving formative assessment strategies into our daily instruction, we can gain continuous insights into student learning and provide timely support. Summative evaluations then serve as meaningful checkpoints to gauge overall progress and achievement.

By shifting our focus from solely relying on marks to embracing assessment as a tool for guiding and enhancing learning, we can create a more supportive and effective environment where every student has the opportunity to truly understand and appreciate the beauty and power of mathematics. This thoughtful and purposeful approach to assessment and evaluation will undoubtedly contribute to more transformative learning experiences for our students.

References

  1. Assessment and Evaluation. (n.d.). Assessment And Evaluation – What Is The Difference? https://icc.edu/faculty-staff/files/Difference-between-Assessment-and-Evaluation.pdf
  2. Knowly. (2020, July 22). Assessment vs evaluation: what's the difference? Easy LMS. https://www.onlineassessmenttool.com/knowledge-center/assessmentknowledge-center/assessment-vs-evaluation/item10642
  3. Next School. (2016, December 15). 6 Problems with Our School System. [Video]. YouTube https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=okpg-lVWLbE
  4. Rethinking classroom assessment with purpose in mind. (2006). Manitoba. https://www.edu.gov.mb.ca/k12/assess/wncp/full_doc.pdf

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
Formative assessment is the secret to real math learning, writes Monica Kochar.

item.title: A mission to improve FLN
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Faiz Mohammad Qureshi

Foundational literacy and numeracy has been acknowledged as a critical imperative in the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020. Various studies (such as the National Achievement Survey, ASER, Pratibha Parv) and experiences have been indicating the necessity of this for a long time now. Following the introduction of NEP 2020, several states have taken positive initiatives in this direction from the academic year 2021-22. These initiatives include infrastructural arrangements, human resources and understanding, curriculum development, textbooks, teacher guides, workbooks, and capacity building of teachers and functionaries.

To address this need the Madhya Pradesh government launched the Mission Ankur programme under the NIPUN Bharat initiative in 2021-22. Here, I give some insights about this well-structured and academically enriched programme.

Programme insights

This programme has been designed to facilitate early literacy and numeracy acquisition. Some of the processes and activities are based on experiential assumptions, while others have been designed according to the specific needs of the programme.

Two key aspects emerge here: first, how children learn, and second, how educators can be guided to implement effective teaching strategies in their classrooms. A critical challenge lies in accurately assessing teachers' capacities and the actual instructional time available in their classrooms. Often, a training session is conducted, followed by classroom implementation, and then a refresher or subsequent training session. However, the content of these training sessions is typically designed by academicians based on limited observation and experience.

In most cases, the training framework for an entire academic year is predetermined and implemented at fixed intervals, aligning the program’s operations with its overarching objectives. To navigate this complexity, real-time data analysis has been integrated into monthly meetings and short-term training sessions. This data-driven approach constitutes a significant strength of this programme.

Availability of materials

Another major feature of the programme is the availability of Teaching Learning Materials (TLM). Ensuring that every school has maximum access to supplementary teaching materials for Hindi, English, and mathematics has been a significant achievement. From an academic perspective, these materials are highly useful and enrich the teaching-learning process.

However, one crucial challenge is ensuring that teachers know how to use these materials effectively. Most teachers are familiar with various teaching methods, and those who need additional guidance are provided training under this programme.

Another concern is that while many teachers know how to use the materials, they struggle to integrate them into their daily lessons or use them as needed – either due to time constraints or classroom management issues. Lastly, the teachers' interest and willingness to use these materials also play a significant role.

Workbooks and teacher handbook: the core of the programme

The most crucial components of this programme are the workbooks and the teacher handbook. For all subjects from grades 1 to 4, every lesson has been structured weekly, with each day's activities clearly described. This is designed to ensure that if the outlined activities are followed, students will learn effectively.

The workbook includes three periodic assessments to assess students' learning levels that help them progress. Each page contains engaging, structured academic exercises. To ensure the effective implementation of these workbooks, the program includes orientation sessions, training, monthly discussions, and a detailed teacher guide that provides step-by-step instructions on how to work on each page.

In addition to this guide, the programme also integrates lesson planning, training on teaching methods, and scaffolding support provided by educational mentors (Jan Shikshak). This comprehensive approach enhances the teaching-learning process.

Teacher autonomy

A significant concern with this programme is regarding teacher autonomy and the assumption that "teachers do not know how to teach." Chapter 3, Page 560 of the National Curriculum Framework 2023 (NCF-SE 23), however, presents a different perspective on teacher autonomy:

"Teachers must have the pedagogic autonomy to plan and organise content, decide the sequence and methods of teaching students as the situation demands, along with ways of assessing their learning ..."

In this context, the extensive structuring of the workbooks appears to be overwhelming for teachers. Teachers are required to ensure that all students complete at least three to four pages per subject per day, review their work, and sign off on it. Managing this alongside other non-academic responsibilities makes the process exhausting. Ultimately, this critical educational activity often gets reduced to mere "filling and completing of workbooks."

Practical challenges in classroom implementation

Another major issue is that students who are irregular in attendance fall behind in completing the workbook pages according to the weekly schedule. In just one or two weeks, the gap between the regular and irregular students widens significantly, forcing those who missed lessons to copy from their classmates. Teachers, too, find themselves facilitating this process because they are required to verify and sign off on each day’s work.

Additionally, teacher availability and time management in schools pose serious challenges. The situation varies – some classrooms have 45-50 students, while others have 25 students across multiple grades (multi-grade teaching). In both cases, classroom management and ensuring personalized attention to each child remain a major concern.

Multi-grade teaching is a reality and it was considered while designing the programme's training modules and workbooks. However, training sessions create artificial classroom conditions (for simulation), making it extremely difficult to implement these strategies in actual classrooms. Moreover, teachers are burdened with administrative tasks such as student verification, ID creation, and scholarships, which further divert their attention from teaching.

Despite these challenges, teachers are striving to help their students achieve foundational literacy and numeracy skills. It is expected that students currently in grade 2 will benefit from this programme by the time they reach grade 5. However, for students currently in grade 4, the question remains: Will teachers continue to use these enriched learning processes and activities even after the closure of the programme by the end of 2027?

Possible improvements

To make this programme more effective, the following suggestions could be considered:

  1. Reduce the length of the workbooks to make them more manageable.
  2. Allow teachers some autonomy in their classrooms, so that they can implement teaching strategies flexibly.
  3. Convert monthly meetings into bi-monthly sessions and make them entirely teacher-led with a pedagogical approach.
  4. Develop a system that helps teachers identify essential instructional practices that might be missing from their classrooms.
  5. Exempt teachers who are handling foundational literacy and numeracy from non-academic tasks, so that they can focus solely on teaching.

If we, as a system, can ensure these improvements, we may successfully achieve the goals of foundational literacy and numeracy within the next 3-4 academic years. Moreover, these refinements will lead to richer educational experiences for future generations.

Faiz Mohammad Qureshi works with the Azim Premji Foundation. He can be reached at faiz.qureshi@azimpremjifoundation.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Faiz Mohammad Qureshi
Can foundational literacy be built when teachers feel overwhelmed and under-trusted? Faiz Mohammad Qureshi writes about Madhya Pradesh’s new FLN initiative.

item.title: The betting app craze in India: a challenge for students, teens, and educators
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

In the digital age, India’s youth are navigating a landscape transformed by technology, where opportunities and risks coexist in unprecedented ways. Among the most pressing challenges emerging today is the rapid rise of online betting apps – a phenomenon that has swept across the country, captivating millions of young people with the allure of quick money and excitement. What is this betting app craze? What is its impact on students, teenagers, and educators? And why is there an urgent need for awareness, education, and regulation to safeguard the future of India’s youth?

The digital gateway to betting began with the explosion of affordable smart phones and cheap internet data that democratized access to online platforms, including betting apps. With just a few taps, young Indians can download apps that allow them to wager on cricket, football, and virtual casino games. These apps boast sleek designs, aggressive marketing, campaigns, and promises of instant financial gains, making them highly appealing to students and teens who often dream of supplementing their income or achieving financial independence quickly. The fantasy of turning a small bet into a life-changing sum is a powerful draw that many find hard to resist.

Cricket, India’s most beloved sport, plays a central role in this trend. Major tournaments like the Indian Premier League (IPL) see a surge in betting activity, with millions placing stakes on every ball bowled. Betting apps leverage this passion by employing celebrity endorsements, sometimes even using AI-generated deepfake videos of cricket icons such as MS Dhoni and Virat Kohli to build trust and attract users. Social media influencers and celebrities further amplify the message, often glamorizing betting as a fun and lucrative pastime.

But there is the dark reality of addiction and financial ruin beneath the glitz and glamour. Many young users fall prey to addiction, lured by the psychological tricks embedded in these apps. The thrill of near-wins, intermittent small payouts, and the dopamine rush from betting create a potent psychological trap. Stories abound of students and young professionals who have lost significant sums – sometimes running into lakhs of rupees – leading to financial distress, academic decline, and strained family relationships.

One poignant example is the case of Ashwini, a 17-year-old from Delhi, who became addicted to online betting apps during the pandemic. What began as casual gaming to redeem reward points quickly escalated into gambling for money. Over the course of a year, Ashwini borrowed from friends, pilfered valuables from home, and even accessed his father’s bank account to fund his habit, losing over ₹2 lakh. When confronted, he initially denied the addiction, but later admitted to his compulsive behaviour. His parents paid off his debts, hoping he would stop, but he resumed betting and became aggressive when denied money. Ashwini was eventually brought to psychiatric treatment for gambling addiction, highlighting the severe mental health and familial consequences of such compulsions.

Many apps operate in legal gray zones or outright illegality, using fake games and manipulated odds to keep users hooked. When users attempt to withdraw their winnings, they often encounter technical glitches, unresponsive customer support, or app shutdowns, leaving them with losses and no recourse. The consequences extend beyond finances. Mental health issues, including anxiety, depression, and in extreme cases, suicidal tendencies, have been linked to betting addiction among the youth. The stigma around gambling often prevents affected individuals from seeking help, exacerbating their plight.

As to the scale and societal impact, the betting app epidemic is vast and growing. Reports indicate that during major sporting events, nearly 370 million Indians engage in online betting, with annual losses estimated at over ₹1.1 lakh crore. Despite government efforts to ban illegal sites, new platforms continue to emerge, often operating from outside India and using cryptocurrencies to evade detection. This not only threatens individual finances but also facilitates organized crime and money laundering.

For students and teens, the impact is particularly acute. Peer pressure, easy access to technology, and the glamorization of betting create a perfect storm. Many young people start betting as a way to make sports more exciting or to earn quick money but soon find themselves trapped in a cycle of compulsion and loss. Academic performance suffers, relationships with family and friends deteriorate, and the risk of mental health crises increases.

What then is the role of educators and schools? Teachers and educational institutions are on the frontline of this challenge. Awareness of the risks of online betting must become integral to school and college curricula. Open conversations can demystify the glamorous facade of betting apps and expose the harsh realities faced by many users. Financial literacy programs should equip students with the skills to manage money wisely and recognize the dangers of quick-money schemes.

Schools can also provide safe spaces for students to discuss their concerns and seek help without the fear of judgment. Educators can collaborate with parents and community leaders to launch awareness campaigns that highlight real-life stories and promote resilience against the lure of betting apps.

As to legal and regulatory responses, India’s legal framework on betting is complex and fragmented, with enforcement challenges exacerbated by the cross-border nature of many apps and the use of cryptocurrencies. The government has intensified efforts to block illegal sites and investigate scams, but experts argue that stronger regulation, better enforcement, and international cooperation are essential to curb the menace effectively.

Stricter Know Your Customer (KYC) norms, monitoring of digital payments, and swift action against illegal operators can protect vulnerable users. However, laws alone are insufficient without comprehensive public education and victim support systems.

What is also needed are robust support systems for victims, including counselling, financial advice, and legal assistance. Breaking the stigma around gambling addiction and creating accessible help channels can encourage affected individuals to seek aid early, preventing further harm.

The betting app craze is more than a passing trend – it is a digital epidemic that threatens the wellbeing of India’s youth. While technology offers immense opportunities, it also poses risks that demand vigilance and responsibility. Students, teens, educators, families, policymakers, and society at large must collaborate to protect the next generation from the seductive but perilous world of online betting.

By fostering awareness, strengthening education, enhancing regulation, and providing compassionate support, we can ensure that India’s digital revolution empowers young people rather than ensnares them in cycles of addiction and loss. The future depends on a collective commitment to turning the tide against this growing challenge.

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A GenZ in spirit, with the benefit of hindsight, she uses her 37 years in media and education to help leverage generational diversity at work and at home. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
Betting apps are luring India’s youth with dreams of easy money. Neerja Singh shares what educators and parents need to know.

item.title: Connecting with language
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ashish Oommen John

(with inputs from Preeti Jangra)

Namaste, Sofia. Namaste, Niya. Namaste Arya.

Namaste.

Namaste.

Kya khaya?

Doodh.

Anda.

Bread.

This is how Preeti’s classes with Arya, Niya, and Sofia would begin. Learning another language can often appear daunting – different rules, different ideas, different ways of talking about something. Language isn’t just something you spend countless hours learning in school in the hope of scraping through an exam. Language is so much more – it has history, culture, tradition, innovation, and emotion. It is also informed by our unique and varied experiences. Language is power and belonging. But, of course, when little children have been asked by their parents to take after-school Hindi lessons, they don’t care about any of this.

Let’s meet the children. Arya is the oldest. When Preeti began working with her, she was nine years old and already had a basic vocabulary and understanding of Hindi. Sofia and Niya, both seven years old, did not speak the language at all. Preeti meets them online once a week for 45 minutes. The children are based in the UK, and while they do encounter Hindi on their visits to India and occasionally through family and friends, they do not have the need to engage with it on a regular basis. As the one with the most enhanced Hindi-speaking ability, Arya routinely lords over her sisters by conversing solemnly with her father in this secret tongue – asking for important things like more butter on her toast.

Play

Preeti’s methodology consists of three elements that blend together – play, conversation, and connection. Each of these will be detailed in this article. We begin with play. As adults, we can sometimes have a sceptical view of play. It can be seen as a waste of time, or at best, as something that may be tolerated, or on occasion, encouraged as a hook in classes, after which we must get down to business. Perhaps, it is the noise that children generate while playing, or just raw jealousy, but we would much rather they didn’t.

But we know that children (and adults) learn a great deal through play. Interpersonal skills, inventiveness, confidence, self-regulation – the list can go on. It is also fun. It takes away the seriousness attributed to learning without impacting the child’s ability to learn. And, of course, it helps them stay interested in what Preeti is trying to teach them.

Preeti uses a wide range of ways to play while teaching Hindi such as creating a story with the children taking turns to add one sentence at a time or dancing to songs in Hindi and learning to count through it. Sometimes, she asks them questions in Hindi and gets the children to draw their responses. The questions could be as simple as, “which is the fastest animal?” or more nuanced as, “if you know that your parents are going to agree to something, what would you ask for?” By encouraging them to draw, she is able to elicit curious responses. To the second question, for example, the children drew computers or electronic devices of some sort. However, to a question about a memory they have of rain, they drew pictures of the food they had eaten while it was raining outside.

The challenges of learning a language are many, but when combined with the fact that Preeti meets the children for one hour per week online, keeping their attention for the entire time is vital. Play interests them enough to continue to be in the class.

Another fan favourite, especially in the early days, was the scavenger hunt. In order to teach them the names of colours, Preeti would name a coulour and the children would scurry off to find things of that colour. It is a wonderful way of ensuring that they don’t have to sit still and stare at a screen for the entire lesson while also listening, translating the word into a language they understand, and then hurrying off to act on this knowledge. This aspect of play, however, would be incomplete without the chance to discuss what they had just done through conversation.

Conversation

The parents of the children had asked Preeti to focus on listening and speaking skills, and conversations are a fantastic way of doing that. Talking to other people is arguably the fundamental use of language. As human beings, we enjoy talking. Conversations afford us a space within which we can use language in creative ways. Whether it is mispronouncing a word in order to make a joke, or using a particular phrase to invoke thought, or revealing fewer details in order to generate curiosity, we instinctively use language to make our lives richer.
