Language is most useful to us when we make it our own, when we can play with it, and infuse it with a part of who we are.

These conversations that Preeti has with the children are intrinsically linked to the aspect of play. Both play and conversation are intertwined and ensure that everybody is participating and also using Hindi to express their thoughts. These activities also come with degrees of nuance. At one level, Preeti asks the children questions and they take time to discuss the answers. Further along the complexity scale, they take stories in Hindi and role-play them. Sometimes, after they have learnt the meaning of a particular word or phrase, the children are asked to try and use it outside of the class. They can do this either in conversation with one another or with their parents.

Preeti also introduced the trio to the Hindi movie Lagaan. Even one movie can make a big difference. I think back to my schooling when I was attempting to learn French. I thought I had a fairly decent command over the language. And then, we watched a movie. Three minutes in, while simultaneously overwhelmed by the language I clearly knew nothing about and also deeply grateful to the inventor of subtitles, a distinct realization swept over me. The people of France did not speak as slowly and clearly as my teacher. For Arya, Sofia, and Niya, watching Lagaan, exposed them to a variety of accents, speech patterns, words, and phrases that Preeti would never have been able to use in class. Through one movie, their overall concept of the Hindi language expanded.

The girls learn a great deal through rhymes as well. These rhymes, about vegetables and animals, add to their vocabulary and allow them access to a large number of words with which they can play. Nani Teri Morni, Bandar Mama, Lakdi ki Kathi, and the letter-specific rhymes from Chandrika Mathur’s Hindi ki Duniyaare some in particular that are used. The students enjoy adding gestures and actions to the rhymes as well, allowing them to engage their entire bodies in the learning of a language. Letter-specific rhymes emphasize one letter of the alphabet by using the sound repeatedly in a variety of words.

N se nana nani pyare,

rehte hai wo nadi kinare,

roj naav ki serrr karate,

neeli nadi ke paar le jaate.

Hindi ki Duniya, Chandrika Mathur

Another way that Preeti engages the children in conversation is by setting up a pretend shop. Everybody takes turns to be both the shopkeeper and customer. The customer enters the shop to buy something. A conversation ensues, perhaps even a negotiation over the price, with one party attempting to get the better of the other. Finally, there is a resolution in one direction and the customer leaves. While this forms the basic format of the encounter, the variables are different every time, and this helps keep it entertaining for the children. The children enjoy putting on accents, doing funny voices, and coming up with the strangest things to ask shopkeepers. And it is here that the beauty of this game lies. Unlike some other games or activities in which the rules must be followed strictly, this is a free-thinking game. This game is only improved by everybody being silly. And when the children are so focused on being silly and having a good time, they are no longer conscious of making mistakes. It is at this moment that they are no longer worried about learning Hindi; instead they are using it to achieve a different end. The wonderful thing is the level of comfort they feel with the language when they stop trying so hard.

It is in conversation that we are able to better understand another person’s ideas, and also our own. It is through conversation that we can truly connect.

Connection

This brings us to the final element of Preeti’s methodology. Connection. What is language if not an attempt at connection? We all seek to connect with other people, with ideas, and with ourselves, and language is what enables it. The teaching of language must account for connection.

Any educator will tell you that it is through connecting with one’s students that one is truly able to teach or learn anything. Without that connection, any teaching-learning process is ultimately futile. This is not to say that a teacher cannot teach and a student cannot learn if they don’t connect. It certainly is possible. Children do not form deep connections with all their teachers, and vice-versa. However, where there is a relationship based on mutual trust and respect, the learning process is made a great deal easier.

There are few things that can be more embarrassing than learning a new language. We stutter, we flounder, we search for words and sounds that are always just out of reach as all notions of self-respect and suave demeanour abandon us. And yet, making mistakes and having the occasional laugh at ourselves is a faster way to develop linguistic ability than the alternative. The role that the teacher plays here, then, is crucial. If a learner has developed a relationship of trust with their teacher, they know that they won’t be ridiculed, singled out, or laughed at for making a mistake. A deep bond between the teacher and learner will go a long way to ensuring that the latter has the freedom to explore, make mistakes, and explore again.

Languages also help us connect with ideas. If a language is a whole world, then learning one is learning to interact with that world. And in interacting with this new world, we gain the tools to reflect on our own – to look at what we already know so well through fresh eyes.

However, one of the most beautiful aspects of learning a language is the ability to find a connection between oneself and the cultures associated with the language. Preeti enjoys engaging her students in discussion about what makes a good teacher, asking them to help her improve as an instructor as well as a human being. This, in turn, helps to ensure that the bond they share keeps growing. Sharing can also bring us closer to strangers at an airport or on a train simply because we know a little bit of their language. Though they might not know it yet, Arya, Sofia, and Niya have embarked on a journey that will afford them a sense of belonging to a community that they might not otherwise have known.

Namaste, Sofia. Namaste, Niya. Namaste, Arya.

Namaste.

Namaste.

Kya khaya?

Maine doodh piya.

Maine anda khaya.

Maine bread aur butter khaya.

Preeti’s classes still begin with namastes and asking about how they are and what they have eaten. Now, they respond in sentences and phrases instead of words. They no longer hesitate. While this might not seem like much, there is a world of difference that has taken place. They have played, chatted, and learnt together. And most of all, they have found ways to connect.

*The names of the children have been changed to preserve their privacy.

**Preeti’s work with three children, over the course of three plus years, has shaped our understanding of how to teach a language to children for whom it is foreign. With Hindi, this applies to children based in other countries (as these three children are) just as much as it does to those in other states of India where it is not widely spoken. We wanted to write about the things that we have learnt so that it may be of use to others undertaking a similar challenge. A part of Preeti’s system comes from the thinking and writing of Chandrika Mathur – in particular, her curriculum called Hindi ki Duniya, and we are grateful to her for it.

Ashish Oommen John works as an educator, thinking about school systems, teaching, teacher education, and library and literacy programmes. Currently, he is working with a school called Asian Christian High School in Hosur, Tamil Nadu. He can be reached at preeti.jangra0304@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ashish Oommen John
What does play have to do with language learning? Ashish Oommen John writes about a teacher who uses games and rhymes to teach children a language they barely knew.

item.title: A simple math puzzle, a big impact
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Mokhtar Zaman

In every school, there are students who love solving problems, students who hesitate to try, and students who just pass by without noticing. But something magical happens when a puzzle appears on the whiteboard near the entrance gate of our school.

Every week, I put up a simple yet thought-provoking math puzzle. It is not something from a textbook. It is a reasoning-based question that even primary students can solve if they think hard enough. And then, I step back and watch.

At first, there is curiosity. A few students stop, tilt their heads, and read the question carefully. Some whisper guesses to their friends, while others pull out their notebooks and write it down to solve later. I have seen students from grades 2 and 3, unsure of the question, discuss it with students of grades 8 or 9, and ask for help. And the best part is that the senior students don’t just give the answer away, they guide the younger students, step by step, explaining how they can think about it.

By lunchtime, the puzzle becomes a topic of discussion. Some students confidently claim they have solved it, while others wait to check their answers before submitting. A locked box in front of the staffroom awaits their responses. It’s funny how they treat this simple activity as if it were a serious mission. They tear small scraps from their notebooks, scribble their answers, fold the papers carefully, and drop them into the box.

A week later, it’s time for the big reveal. I unlock the box and go through the answers – some right, some wrong, but all filled with effort. As I separate the correct answers, I imagine the students’ eager faces, waiting to see if their name will be displayed on the board.

And then, the best moment arrives. Pictures of the students who got the answer right go up on the board. You should see their reactions!

They rush to the board, calling their friends, pointing at their names. Even those who didn’t get it right this time look determined, already asking when the next puzzle will be up.

Why this matters

This isn’t just about math. It’s about excitement, curiosity, and the joy of learning.

  1. It makes students think. Instead of memorizing formulas, they learn to approach problems logically, step by step.
  2. It creates a culture of learning. Younger students get guidance from seniors and discussions happen outside the classroom, making learning natural.
  3. It builds patience. The waiting period before the results are revealed teaches students that answers don’t always come instantly.
  4. It gives a sense of achievement. When their names with their photos go up on the board, their confidence grows. They feel proud, motivated, and ready for the next challenge.
  5. It makes school exciting. Instead of just following a routine, students look forward to something unexpected, something that sparks their curiosity.

Week after week, a new puzzle appears, and the cycle begins. And just like that, math stops being a subject – it becomes a part of their everyday excitement, a small challenge that makes their school days a little more interesting.

Mokhtar Zaman teaches at Azim Premji School, Dhamtari. He can be reached at mokhtar.zaman@azimpremjifoundation.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mokhtar Zaman
A simple puzzle on a whiteboard near the school gate is changing how students feel about math. Mokhtar Zaman’s piece shows what joyful learning can look like.

item.title: Understanding by design: a framework for effective education
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Roopa Vinayak Ram

Social media has crystallized into an all-important public utility – this is what writer and journalist Johnann Hari says in his book, Stolen Focus-Why you can’t pay attention and deeply think again. Instead of receiving meaningful information, we are often overwhelmed by an endless onslaught of notifications – a steady trickle of 'behavioural cocaine' – telling us of the number of likes or comments received, or upcoming celebrations, and other candy floss, all of which impair our competency to focus and think clearly.

Teaching screenagers has become an arduous task for teachers today. Yes, you read that right, screenagers, that’s the term that rightly describes students who are raised on smartphones and social media. These digital natives have become seasoned scrollers and loyal snackers of bite-sized content such that the onus is on teachers to innovate and hook their attention, converting complex concepts into immersive experiences. But a critical question lingers: what can teachers do to build a culture of hard work to assimilate and achieve mastery in subjects, especially when the matter is about students who are primed for instant gratification and snackable knowledge?

Is there any solution?

This persistent challenge that teachers face can be addressed by an educational framework called Understanding by Design (UbD) conceived by Grant Wiggins and Jay McTighe, two widely recognized educators and authors from USA. Wiggins and McTighe have spent a major part of their careers advocating meaningful educational reforms. In this article, I attempt to illustrate how the UbD model can be used to teach students so that those who struggle with a subject start to understand it. I will be using examples from accountancy to unravel the framework; but, this strategy can be applied to any subject and is therefore useful to all teachers.

The UbD framework: an overview

UbD at its core, is a curriculum design framework that emphasizes backward design. In contrast to conventional pedagogical planning, where the focus is on starting with activities or lessons, UbD begins with teachers identifying the end learning outcome and then working backwards to create lessons, activities, and assessments that support those outcomes. In other words, UbD is all about visualizing the big picture and then taking steps to achieve that big picture.

As Stephen R. Covey in his book, The Seven Habits of Highly Effective People, rightly pointed out “To begin with the end in mind means to start with a clear understanding of your destination. It means to know where you are going so that you better understand where you are now so that the steps you take are always in the right direction.”

What does the framework look like?

The framework consists of three stages:

  1. Identify desired results: What should students know, understand, and be able to do by the end of the unit?
  2. Determine acceptable evidence: How do I assess whether students have achieved those outcomes?
  3. Plan learning experiences and instruction: What activities, resources, and teaching strategies will help students meet their learning goals?

How can UbD be implemented by accounting teachers?

Step 1: Identify the desired results in accounting

  • Understanding the fundamental accounting equation (Assets = liabilities + owner's equity).
    • Applying the concept of double-entry bookkeeping.
    • Knowing how to record basic transactions in a journal.
    • Posting transactions so recorded into appropriate ledgers.
    • Converting these ledgers into a Trial Balance, which is the first step to preparing financial statements.
    • And finally, preparing and interpreting these financial statements (income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement) to come to a meaningful conclusion regarding the profitability and financial health of the given business.

At this stage, teachers should identify what they want their students to understand, not just know. For example, students should not only be able to create a balance sheet, but should also understand why the balance sheet is structured the way it is and how it provides useful information to business owners, investors, and other stakeholders.

Step 2: Determine acceptable evidence

This is the stage where teachers decide how they will assess their students to check how well they have understood the concepts taught. The conventional assessment methods such as quizzes or standardized exams might not reveal deep understanding. It is here that the UbD framework comes in handy. UbD urges teachers to use performance tasks that require students to apply their knowledge in real-world contexts.

For example, one method of assessment could be asking students to prepare financial statements when presented with a set of business transactions. To make the assessment more meaningful, the teacher could ask the students to explain their reasoning for each entry and the implications for the business's financial health. This demonstrates not only technical proficiency, but also a deeper understanding of how accounting information is used in decision-making.

Stage 3: Plan learning experiences and instruction

Once the desired outcomes and assessments have been established, the teacher can move on to planning the actual lessons and activities. This is where accounting, as a hands-on subject, can really benefit from the UbD framework. By focusing on engaging real-world applications, teachers can make accounting more accessible and interesting for students.

Applying UbD to teaching accounting: practical examples

Now that we have a basic understanding of how UbD works, let’s explore specific ways in which the framework can be applied to teach accounting.

Example 1: Understanding the accounting equation

One of the first concepts students learn in accounting is the accounting equation: Assets=liabilities+owner's equity. While this equation sounds direct and simple, its inferences are not always immediately obvious to beginners.

UbD application

Desired outcome: Students should not only know the constituents of the accounting equation but also understand its implications in the context of a business.

Assessment: Have students analyze a small business's financial situation. Give them a case study with a set of transactions where they have to identify the accounts involved, classify them into assets, liabilities, or equity. Once this is done, they should also explain how changes in one part of the equation affect the other.

Learning activity: A hands-on simulation exercise where students role-play as managers of a fictional company. Each student is given a role (e.g., inventory manager, sales manager) and must record transactions related to their role. As a group, they will then prepare a balance sheet based on their entries, discussing how each transaction affects the overall equation.

A simulation activity such as this not only reinforces the concept of the accounting equation but also makes the process tangible for students. Learning by doing helps students strengthen their understanding of theoretical concepts.

Example 2: Double-entry bookkeeping

Double-entry bookkeeping is another core concept in accounting, but it can appear complex to beginners. The concept of every transaction having two sides or entries – one on the debit side and one on the credit side – can feel conflicting at first.

UbD application

Desired outcome: Students should be able to not just explain why the double-entry system ensures accuracy in financial records but also explicitly record transactions using the double-entry system.

Assessment: Ask students to complete a set of transactions for a small business, making both debit and credit entries for each transaction. They must also provide a brief written explanation of how the double-entry system prevents errors in accounting.

Learning activity: Use a hands-on, game-based approach where students participate in a "transaction relay." In this activity, one student reads out a transaction (e.g., "The company buys an office computer for ` 50,000 on credit"), and the rest of the class sprints to record the correct debit and credit entries. Points are awarded for accuracy and speed, and the class discusses any missteps taken along the way.

When bookkeeping is turned into a game, it is easier to keep students engaged. By repeating the same game with different types of transactions, the rules of double-entry system of bookkeeping are strengthened.

Making accounting easy for beginners through UbD

By using the UbD framework, accounting teachers can customize their instructions to help students learn the basics of accounting and develop their ability to think critically and apply their knowledge in real-world contexts. Here are some additional strategies to make accounting easier for beginners:

  • Incorporate visuals: Diagrams, flowcharts, and tables can help students understand the relationships between different accounting concepts, such as how transactions flow through the accounting cycle.
  • Team-based learning: Group activities and projects encourage peer learning and help students develop a deeper understanding of the material through discussion and collaboration.
  • Constant feedback: Regular formative assessments can help students identify areas where they need to improve before they move on to more complex topics.
  • Everyday examples: Incorporating real-world case studies, guest speakers from the accounting industry or even fieldtrips to local businesses can help students see the relevance of accounting in the world around them.

Conclusion

Throughout their book, Understanding by Design, McTighe and Wiggins present abundant examples, workable strategies, and blueprints to guide educators in implementing the Understanding by Design approach. The complexities of teachers' work and the related oversights that can shroud their minds are plenty, and a framework such as UbD can come in handy. Through interesting, real-world activities, timely feedback, and group activities, learning can become both navigable and pleasant for students.

Roopa Vinayak Ram is committed to creating productive learning environments in schools. She is an accounts teacher at National Academy For Learning, Bengaluru. She can be reached at roopa304@yahoo.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Roopa Vinayak Ram
Through games, role-play, and real-world challenges, the UbD model can make any subject come alive. Roopa Vinayak Ram shows how even learning accounting can be hands-on.

item.title: It’s all in the mud
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Meenakshi Umesh

The natural learning process of every child involves curiosity as the fundamental drive. If children are born to learn, what is the role of a learning space or school? This was the challenge I faced in 2000 when I decided to homeschool my children. And I responded by trusting the process. We can create anything tangible by using the resources of the planet and the efforts of our body. The involvement of the body in the process of meaningful creation of usable resources without the by product of waste is to live sustainably and serve the ecosystem too! The simplicity of life dawned on us. And the richness in that simplicity baffled us! Why then are we all so stressed about our children’s education? Because we have complicated life!

At Puvidham, creation is a way of life. We are all involved in physical work to satisfy our basic needs. At one point, the 5th and 6th level children felt the need for a playhouse, and the whole school involved itself in its construction. The knowledge we gained in the process was immense. While it might seem like all play, we encountered math, science, life skills and more in the one month we took to build the playhouse. I understand not all schools have the luxury of space, but I hope this project will encourage teachers to find ways in which their children can work with mud.

Discussion and free expression of thought (language and math)

  1. Establishing the need: We established a need to learn to work with mud as a construction material and offered the possibility of creating some space for the children’s use. Why should we build with mud? What other materials can be used? Why should we build it ourselves?
  2. Deciding the size: The next step was to decide how big this space would be. Since at 10 years, children do not have an idea of measurement in feet or meters, we proposed that they decide how many children should be able to sit inside the playhouse at any given time.
  3. Arriving at areas: Then the children had to measure the space needed by one person to sit, stand, and move a little bit. They were asked to outline the space needed for one person to sit comfortably, turn freely, and walk around. They split into groups of five and used chalk to mark on the floor the space required by each one in the group to sit, turn, and walk around. We made squares of one foot by one foot on a chart paper and used them to determine how many square feet of area was needed.

Measurement and calculations of material and space (math, science, and sustainability)

  1. Arriving at a size: Each group learnt how to use a measuring tape. On an average, it was decided that 5 feet x 5 feet space was needed for each person inside the playhouse, so the playhouse was now a big house of 25x5=125 square feet.
  2. Affordability and moderation: At this time, we sat down to calculate how much money it would cost us to build a space this size. What would the materials be? How will we do the roofing? How can we minimize the size to reduce the cost? After a lot of discussion, we arrived at 10 feet by 8 feet space.
  3. Materials and form: We decided to build the playhouse with waste bricks and mud as both are almost free of cost. To make an enclosed space with mud, we decided to make a dome so that we wouldn’t need wood, which can both be attacked by termites and lead to cost escalation.

Visualizing and finalizing the shape (geometry and design)

  1. Drawing the form and understanding scale: A rectangle was drawn on a graph paper. The children wanted 10 feet length and arrived at 8 feet width to minimize the space. When the wall thickness of 9 inches was drawn, the children realized that the area was increasing. During discussion, it became apparent that corners are useless, as no one will sit there. So, to further reduce the area, they decided to round off the corners. Since the width was 8 feet, a radius of 4 feet was used to draw two semicircles on both ends of the rectangle. They found out the area of the space by counting the squares on the graph paper. (The formula for calculating the area of a circle was used and the areas were cross-checked.)
  2. Finalizing the form: This capsule shape we felt could easily be constructed with bricks and mud. Using the centre of the two semicircles we could construct a semicircular dome and join it at the top to make the roof. But this would mean that the height of the playhouse would only be 5 feet.
  3. Ergonomics and entrance design: The groups then did a study of the heights of the children in their class and agreed to a door height of 3.5 feet. Though most of them were around 4 feet 6 inches tall, they felt that they could bend a little to enter the play space and decided to build an arched doorway. A circular opening on the opposite side would act as a window.

Bringing our drawings to life (math)

  1. Making a complete set of drawings: After making the drawing on the graph paper, we had to learn how to draw using geometric tools and understand the geometry of the structure. Each child had to make the drawings separately using their geometry sets. They started by learning how to draw perfect right angles, how to ensure that the rectangle is perfect, how to mark the centre of the semicircle and then how to draw it, and how to mark the door and window in the plan. They also learnt how to make the elevations and understood how we would construct it. The walls were straight for 1.5 feet and then they curved inward to close into a dome. The drawing took three days of working two hours every day before everyone was able to draw the plan, sections, and elevations.
  2. Calculating the number of bricks needed: To calculate the number of bricks needed, we had to know the perimeter of the structure we were building. So, each group tried to get an idea of the perimeter with the help of the 7th and 8th class mentors. We had to help them understand the surface area of the playhouse and the surface area of one brick with mud mortar. The division was done and the number of bricks was arrived at.

Getting down to work (language, geography, and math)

  1. Deciding the location of the play space: The children wanted to have the play space away from the classrooms. But the adults expressed concern that they would not be able to keep an eye on the children. The children wanted no supervision and the adults were concerned about safety. It was agreed that if for one week we had no complaints about the 5th and 6th class children, then we could tuck away the play space, but if any one made even a single complaint against them, the play space would be closer to the classrooms. Even before we started to build the playhouse, however, there were issues about who could use the playhouse and when. So finally, it was agreed that the playhouse had to be in the vicinity of the classrooms, so that the adults could intervene in case of a serious issue.
  2. Marking on the site: When the children were drawing the plan on the graph paper, it was relatively easy. But marking the structure on land needed a different set of tools. Using twine and golden triangle to mark the rectangle and then the centres of the semicircles and use a twine and stick as a compass to mark the curve was a very interesting transition.

Testing the mud (geography and science)

  1. Sedimentation test: We filled 10 cms of the mud in a transparent bottle and mixed it with water till it dissolved. We left it to settle overnight. The mud settled in layers. The mud profile was drawn by the children and the percentage of the sediment was determined by measuring the thickness of each layer. We found that the mud had three percent clay.
  2. Mud cake test: The children took some mud in one hand, pressed it down with the other, and turned their palms upside down. Depending on the time the mud cake took to fall off, the tensile strength of the mud was determined.
  3. Cylinder roll test: The mud mixture was rolled into a long cylinder. The longer the cylinder, the more the tensile strength.
  4. Ball test: Two mud balls of the same size were made and left to dry. One in the shade and the other in the sun. The cracks on the ball told us about the bonding property of the mortar.
  5. Impact test: The balls were then dropped from a height of 10-12 feet to check the brittleness of the mud and how it would perform under compression.

Improving mud quality (biology and microbiology)

  1. The ratio of mud, sand, and water: Mud, sand, and water were mixed. The children saw how the mud felt and then covered it and left it to ferment for 24 hours. The next day they touched the mud mixture again and saw the difference in pliability.
  2. Fermentation and microbes: Why do people use fermented mud for construction? It improves the plasticity and binding properties due to proper soaking of all particles in the mud. Mycelium, a fungus that is there in all kinds of mud grows and spreads through the mud mixture and increases its binding property.
  3. Plants used to improve the plasticity of the mud: Plants like aloevera, neem, tulsi, milk weed, nirgundi, etc., were collected, crushed, and added to a drum of water. One kilogram of each of the leaves was used.
  4. Kitchen herbs: Three kilograms of Kadukkai seeds were pounded and made into a powder. This was added to 200 litres of water in a plastic drum. Five kilograms of Jaggery was added to this. A quarter of a kilogram of turmeric powder, ½ kilogram of fenugreek seed powder, and one kilogram maida flour made into a gruel with 10 litres of water were all added to a drum. This mixture was stirred everyday for one week to ferment it. This water was used to turn the mud kept ready for construction. This was done to improve insect and termite resistance quality of the mud.

Once the marking was done, and the mud, sand, and water ratio determined, the whole school got involved in the project. Everyone worked for two hours after school everyday to transport stones for the foundation and bricks and mortar for the walls. The 7th and 8th level and 5th and 6th level kids took the lead and they became the masons; all the others were unskilled labour. Some were supplying drinking water or making juice and providing snacks.

In 20 days, the basic structure was ready. The plastering on the inside was done by the 3rd and 4th level kids and the 7th and 8th kids did the water proofing with lime mortar on the outside.

When it was ready, this wonderful structure became everyone’s favourite space.

When children are trusted to follow their inner compass in a non-judgemental space, and performance is not evaluated through exams, they naturally blend freedom with accountability, and this balance leads to meaningful, joyful, and creative learning that remains with them as beautiful memories not by rote but because of the experience and joy.

Making your own mud constructions

This type of a structure can even be built on a terrace for children as a space to read or play. Moving materials can be fun when we need to use physics of simple machines to pull the materials up using pulleys. A tour for collecting plants and herbs to mix with the mud will increase children's sense of wonder at the contribution of plants in traditional construction with mud.

The topic of mud construction can be connected with many subjects.

Geography: Soil formation, soil types, rock types, soil colours, topography of India.

History: History of early man and the ancient civilizations and old structures.

Science: Fermentation of the soil and the role of mycelium in increasing the strength of the soil. Soil structure and content, soil Ph, etc. Use of machines to transport material, work and effort, and pulleys and inclined plane.

Math: Area of all shapes, dimensions of shapes and their perimeter, volume of material needed to build, calculation of number of bricks, stones, etc.

Language (mother tongue): Children can document this project in their journals. At Puvidham, our children wrote in their mother tongue about each day –

what they did, what they understood, and what tomorrow might bring.
They wrote of the sun on their backs, the weight in their arms,
the longing to rest – and the quiet strength that carried them forward,
The eagerness to see the playhouse completed.
They wrote of arguments that flared,
differences that softened and gave way,
and the tender thread of togetherness woven through it all!

Language (English): Anther exercise is to translate what they write in their mother tongues into English. At Puvidham, the children translated their own sentences into English and the facilitator helped them to make it into a comprehensive journal in English.

Meenakshi Umesh started Puvidham Learning Centre, based on Gandhi’s Nai Talim after she decided to move to Dharmapuri, a village far away from Mumbai. Her move came after she realized that city life was harming her and her children. She can be reached at director.meenakshi@puvidham.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Meenakshi Umesh
Students built a real mud playhouse and learned about geometry and biology in real time. Meenakshi Umesh writes about the joyful learning that occurs when children get their hands dirty.

item.title: The messy symphony of learning: rethinking the silent classroom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Tarannum Shaikh

Remember the golden rule of kindergarten, ‘sharing is caring’? Apparently, that heartwarming principle gets shoved into a locker when the first-grade bell rings. Suddenly, classrooms morph into hushed zones where teachers become the sole source of knowledge, barking out instructions while students passively absorb information. Gone are the days of collaborative block-building projects and vibrant finger-painting sessions. Now, the mere thought of sharing a snack sends shivers down some teachers' spines. Imagine this scenario: a child, eyes wide, reaches for a bite of their friend’s idly. "Don't put your hands in others' tiffins [lunchbox]," the teacher scolds, effectively shutting down the possibility of social interaction and a potential lesson in generosity. Her justification? A fear of "mess" and prioritizing order over social interaction. Another child, eager to express themselves creatively, asks to borrow a crayon. "Why don't you bring your own crayon box?" comes the reply, nipping collaboration in the bud. "No, don't ask others, colour it at home," the teacher insists, fostering a sense of isolation rather than encouraging resourcefulness and turn-taking. These seemingly insignificant exchanges highlight a troubling trend – a classroom environment terrified of a little "chaos."

Why are we so afraid of a little noise and a few crumbs? This rigid approach might seem efficient, with teachers maintaining control and students appearing focused. Teachers crave order and a quiet learning environment. This response highlights a crucial point: teachers prioritize order over social interaction. But why? There are several factors at play. Firstly, teachers are often under pressure to cover a specific curriculum within a limited timeframe. Disruptions caused by sharing food or arguing over crayons can eat into valuable instructional time, putting them behind schedule. Secondly, maintaining a controlled environment can feel essential for classroom management. Unstructured interactions, like sharing snacks, can lead to unexpected situations – spills, arguments, or a domino effect of other children wanting to share. While these situations might seem manageable to us, for a teacher juggling a room full of energetic youngsters, the potential for chaos can be daunting. The "no sharing" rule becomes a preventative measure, a way to nip potential disruptions in the bud. Finally, some teachers might simply be less comfortable with fostering a more social learning environment. They might feel more confident delivering direct instruction and may not have the training or experience to effectively navigate the messiness of social interactions within the classroom setting. In their minds, enforcing a "no sharing" rule ensures a smooth flow of instruction, even if it comes at the cost of stifling valuable social interaction. While this approach might achieve a semblance of order in the short term, it could hinder children's social development in the long run. Sharing experiences, even messy ones, allows children to build relationships, practice empathy, and learn conflict resolution skills – all crucial tools for navigating the social world.

Imagine a classroom alive with sound: whispers of crayon trades, negotiations over the last juice box, maybe even a heated debate over the coveted red building block. This "chaos," as some might call it, is actually a goldmine for social development. Research by Kostelnik et al. (2011), tells us why. Social interaction during play is a breeding ground for essential skills – empathy, communication, and conflict resolution. Picture a disagreement over a shared colouring book. Kids learn to express themselves, listen to each other, and ultimately find a solution (sharing maybe or taking turns). This messy process, far from disruptive, is a social learning masterclass. Here's the science behind the mess: research by Pellegrini and Bjorklund (2008) suggests that overly sanitised environments might hinder, not help, development. Play, with its inherent messiness and need for negotiation, allows children to experiment with different roles, resolve conflicts (peacefully or otherwise), and build friendships – all crucial tools for navigating the social world. Sharing a crayon might lead to a squabble over colour, but it also teaches turn-taking and compromise – essential for future success. Adding to the evidence, Darling-Hammond et al. (2009) suggest that fostering collaboration and student interaction can actually lead to deeper learning. In other words, by embracing some playful "disruption", we might be setting our children up for better social and academic success. Yet, the idea of accepting this disruption as part of the learning process can be a hard pill to swallow. Teachers often find themselves shushing and scolding students for talking and sharing.

One funny incident, however, perfectly illustrates the contradiction! After witnessing such corrections day after day, as part of my preschool research on how biases and social attitudes get formed at an early stage, I came across a sight that stopped me in my tracks. One day, I saw all kindergarteners and their teacher on the playground. Have you ever seen a bunch of kindergarteners unleashed on a soccer field for the first time? Forget fancy footwork and intricate plays – for them, it's pure, unadulterated joy. Kicking that round ball around is way more fun than memorizing offside rules or strategizing for victory. They chase it with gleeful abandon, a temporary truce declared as they all reach for the coveted sphere. But here's the kicker (pun intended): imagine their surprise when the teacher, initially excited about some playtime, blows the whistle and shouts, "When will you learn to share?!"

It's a question that leaves you scratching your head. After all, these same kids were told that "no sharing" applies to everything from lunchboxes to crayons. So how, in this chaotic sea of tiny legs and boundless energy, are they supposed to magically develop the art of turn-taking and cooperation? Isn't this a classic case of mixed messages? But there's a disconnect, isn't there? At home, in many upper-middle-class and even lower-middle-class families, children are showered with love and gifts. They often don't experience the need to share because their desires are readily fulfilled. School, too, reinforces this by sometimes prohibiting sharing due to concerns about mess or disruption. So, the question remains: how exactly are we expecting them to learn this crucial social skill?

It's time to rethink the silent classroom. Instead of "no sharing" edicts, what if we created spaces where children learn through interaction? What if, instead of stifling interaction with rigid rules against talking or sharing, we created spaces where children learn through collaboration and social engagement? Research by Rubin et al. (2010) suggests this very approach holds the key. Their findings show that social play acts as a breeding ground for essential life skills – empathy, communication, and conflict resolution. Unlike situations with high stakes (like a test or presentation), play allows for trial and error without significant consequences. This allows children to develop empathy by taking on different roles and perspectives during play. They practice communication by negotiating rules, expressing desires, and resolving disagreements. Conflict resolution specifically benefits from play as children encounter situations where they need to compromise, share, and find solutions that work for everyone involved. These are precisely the abilities our children need to navigate a world that increasingly demands strong social and emotional intelligence.

This doesn't mean abandoning structure altogether. Boundaries are still important. However, consider the power of student-led learning, an approach championed by John Dewey (1938). Dewey, a prominent American philosopher and educational reformer, believed that learning happens best through hands-on experience within a social context. Imagine a classroom where students collaborate to create their own rules for playtime or take turns cleaning up after lunch (think of the life skills learned!). This "messy" process of working together, as some might perceive it, is actually a chance for them to develop problem-solving abilities and a sense of responsibility. Similarly, taking turns cleaning up after lunch fosters not only practical skills but also builds a sense of community and shared purpose, mirroring Dewey's emphasis on learning within a social context. A social context, as Dewey saw it, is an environment where students interact with each other, share ideas, and grapple with problems together. This collaborative effort in cleaning up after lunch isn't just about wiping tables; it's about students learning to see themselves as part of a larger group with shared goals. They contribute to the wellbeing of the classroom as a whole, fostering a sense of belonging and responsibility. This social engagement is central to Dewey's theory, as he believed it allows students to test their ideas, refine their understanding, and develop essential social skills. By allowing them to take ownership of their classroom environment, we're not just teaching them to clean, we're laying the foundation for them to become active and engaged participants in their communities.

Imagine a classroom transformed. Gone are the rows of silent desks, replaced by a vibrant learning space, where students huddle together on colourful rugs. Sunlight streams through windows as laughter mingles with the murmur of discussion. In this picture-perfect scene, students are no longer passive recipients of information, but active participants in shaping their learning environment. The teacher sits cross-legged on the floor, not at the head of the class, but alongside her students. Together, they're finalizing a set of classroom guidelines collaboratively created through lively debate and negotiation. One student proposes a "quiet corner" for independent reading, while another suggests a designated space for collaborative projects. The teacher facilitates the discussion, ensuring everyone has a voice and that the rules established are fair and achievable. This student-led approach fosters a sense of ownership and responsibility. The rules aren't imposed from above, but rather a product of their collective effort. As a result, students are more invested in following them. Healthy social interaction becomes the norm, as students navigate disagreements with the tools they've learned through guided discussions. Teacher remains a constant presence, not as a disciplinarian, but as a supportive guide, ready to offer gentle nudges and encouragement when needed. This is the essence of a student-centered classroom – a place where learning flourishes through collaboration, social engagement, and a shared sense of purpose.

So, let's embrace a little "creative chaos." It might just be the secret weapon our children need to thrive in a world that demands strong social and emotional intelligence. By fostering interaction and collaboration, we can transform classrooms from silent rooms into vibrant social hubs, equipping our children with the skills they need to not only succeed academically, but also become well-rounded individuals who can navigate the complexities of the future.

References

  1. Darling-Hammond, L., & Richardson, N. (2009). Teacher learning: What matters. Educational Leadership, 66(8), 46-53.
  2. Dewey, J. (1938). Experience and education. Kappa Delta Pi.
  3. Kostelnik, M. J., Soderman, A. W., & Whiren, G. P. (2011). Developmentally appropriate curriculum: Best practices in early education (7th ed.). Pearson Education.
  4. Pellegrini, A. D., & Bjorklund, D. F. (2008). The role of sociodramatic play in children's cognitive development. European Journal of Psychology of Education, 23(4), 453-470.
  5. Rubin, K. H., Fein, G. G., & Vandenberg, B. (2010). Play for keeps: The importance of play in childhood development. Oxford University Press.

Tarannum Shaikh is a Research Associate at Azim Premji University, exploring how prejudices emerge in early childhood. She has nine years of experience in IT and has a background in activity-based pedagogy in education. She holds a master's in early childhood education and is passionate about inclusive, research-informed educational practices. She can be reached at tarannum.shaikh@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Tarannum Shaikh
By banning snacks and the sharing of stationery among students, are we also banning real learning? Tarannum Sheik asks whether our fear of classroom chaos may be hindering children’s social skills.

item.title: Transforming a reluctant reader in six days
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

Have you struggled to get your child/student to read? Especially in today’s WhatsApp era, where everything that needs to be said is to be done in as few words as possible?

If you have, you might enjoy this true story. Better still, you may find that this anecdote can initiate a brainstorming session in your own school.

If you can use this episode in some way in your own working environment, then the purpose of this column will be fulfilled!

It was during the COVID pandemic that the principal of a school noticed something on her virtual rounds. One boy was consistent in his resistance to read: he always claimed to have an internet connection with very poor bandwidth. [He didn’t seem to have a problem with his internet when he was not called out to read, however.]

The principal spoke to the boy concerned and requested him to send her an audio recording daily by 5 pm, of him reading a short extract aloud. Barring an initial tardiness, the recordings began dropping into her WhatsApp daily and voila! within six days, she noticed a significant improvement in his vocabulary as well as his self-confidence. When the principal shared the read-aloud recording with the boy’s English teacher, asking her to guess who this boy was, the teacher was unable to do so!

Clearly, we teachers miss the hidden potential of some of our students – human as we are!

The Principal then reflected on the root cause for this speedy success.

In a half hour clip (please see the link at the end of this article), she describes the nature of her instructional intervention. Aimed at a specific change, an instructional intervention can be formalized – and it will often demand follow-up. In this case, the principal systematically followed up with the student for about a week, and was very satisfied with the results.

There is more to this story. When this principal shared her experience with her colleagues who were all part of an online group, they reflected collectively on the possible reasons for her success. The following questions were discussed:

[In order to get a full picture, you will need to watch this clip. I am capturing the essence of the discussion here.]

How did this transformation happen so quickly? The principal shared how she often conveys to students that they need to alter their attitude – if she feels that is needed. What enabled the principal to get such a simple idea and why didn’t it strike the teacher concerned? When – if at all – does a harried teacher get ideas like the principal got, to tackle such a problem? The importance of loosening one’s grip on a problem and brainstorming with colleagues emerged during this online discussion. As for the time that a teacher (imagines she) needs to invest with students individually, the group reflected on whether or not a huge investment of time was truly needed.

Further, during the online class, as the boy’s father passed by, he heard his son’s name being mentioned several times. This triggered his interest, and quickly drew him into the practice of getting his son to read with interest. This elicited the question from the group: Do we always need to involve parents in such interventions? What if the parents are themselves not literate and the child is a first-generation learner? Will such support even be possible then?

The online discussion concluded with a description of how Helen Keller learnt her first abstract word: and remember, abstract words are hard enough for a child with all faculties to master! Helen Keller was a blind, deaf, and mute child: the account of how she learnt abstract words capped the discussion and set the teachers thinking.

Do write in and let us know if this month’s story set you thinking, too!

Watch the half-hour clip here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hftOrj4abOs&t=62s

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
A principal helped her student, a reluctant reader, speak confidently in six days! Neeraja Raghavan writes about the power of thoughtful interventions.

item.title: How flexible is our Constitution?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: What would the opposite of federalism be? I can imagine what the structure of the government might be in a small country that does not have many provinces or states, and so a single government would be enough. I am trying to imagine how such a governance structure would look like in India. Did the Constituent Assembly consider this possibility? Why did they consider a federal constitution?

Anubhuti: The word you are looking for is unitary – such a constitution would suggest the kind of government you are thinking of. Yes, this did come up during numerous debates, but the Assembly did what suits our nation and its history, rather than consider one or the other categories of constitutions. In a final address to the assembly, President Dr. Rajendra Prasad said, “We are not bound to have a constitution which completely and fully falls in line with known categories of constitutions in the world. We have to take certain facts of history in our own country and the Constitution has not, to an inconsiderable extent, been influenced by such realities as facts of history.”[1] It seems he was saying that we could call it a federal constitution in retrospect after the drafting of the Constitution rather than presume federalism from the beginning.

Ira: This reference to historical facts which have been considered in drafting the Constitution is interesting. Does that mean that as situations change, the Constitution will also have to be modified? That sounds paradoxical! The Constitution would have to say that it is final such that it is adopted by everyone, and at the same time also say that it can be changed. How can they imagine what kind of changes are allowed and what are not?

Anubhuti: Brajeshwar Prasad, a member of the Constituent Assembly said the same thing during the debates. Referring to its federal nature, he said, “It is ordinary commonsense that should tell us that if we want to abolish landlordism you cannot seek the consent of the landlord. … The purpose of amending a constitution, in effect, will be to take more powers from the hands of the State Governments and confer them on the Centre.”[2]

If we give that power to the central government, it does amount to being a unitary constitution. Prasad saw this problem and said if a conflict between provincial and central governments should arise, the Constitution should suggest a referendum – put any controversial amendment to vote to the entire population. He gave convincing reasons justifying a referendum – it would be an appeal to the people and emphasize their sovereignty; it would control the representative governments, cure defects in the party system of democracy, and curb the absolutism of a party that possesses a parliamentary majority.[3]

Ira: This would have complicated the issue because they would either have to (a) give the centre more power and veer towards a unitary constitution or (b) adopt an expensive process of voting for amendments.

How did they decide on the amendment process then? There must have been some principles that drove the discussion further. I know we did not choose a referendum because there have been 106 amendments to the Constitution in the last 75 years, but no referendum.

Anubhuti: I read some parts of the Constituent Assembly debates when this discussion happened, and the alternative was also suggested by Brajeshwar Prasad. He said that we could insert a rule that for any amendment, there should be two-thirds majority agreement in both the Lok Sabha (lower house) and the Rajya Sabha (upper house). His argument was that members of the Rajya Sabha will not want to give more power to the central government since they represent federalism and bring in opinions from all states.

He further emphasized that the Constitution should be made flexible and not rigid. His argument was that the Assembly’s vision was blurred by the effect of the partition and so they were not able to take an objective view of the needs of a developing economy.

H.V. Kamath, on the other hand, began his response with a fundamental issue – the need to define what an amendment meant. He was concerned that ‘amendment’ might be interpreted as variation, or even repeal of the Constitution itself.

Ira: That would be disconcerting. Is amendment defined in our Constitution?

Anubhuti: Yes, it is. Article 368 of the Constitution says, “Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, Parliament may in exercise of its constituent power amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of this Constitution in accordance with the procedure laid down in this article.” See, the text solves the paradox you mentioned earlier – how does a constitution call itself flexible and amendable. It defines ‘amendment’ as changing or withdrawing any particular provision in the Constitution, rather than the Constitution itself.

H. V. Kamath further explained two critical reasons for making the Constitution flexible through an easier process of amendment. He said that though the Constitution is a product of representatives of people across India, and they were all wise and educated, they should not associate any superiority to the Constitution they drafted. The reason he gave was that in spite of all this, they were indirectly elected and that too on a restricted franchise. Future parliaments, on the other hand, would be elected on universal adult franchise and so would be better in that they would truly represent the entire population.

Secondly, he said, we should allow for the fact that society would progress with time, and “If the Constitution holds up, blocks, the future progress of our country” by disallowing any amendment or making the procedure difficult, “(If it does not bend), people will break it,” which was not desirable.[4]

Ira: Isn’t it remarkable how our Constitution remains safe and federal, even though it is 75 years now and 106 amendments have been made? Madhav Khosla’s point about the Constitution being a pedagogical tool that will teach us democracy seems to be working.

Anubhuti: Yes that is true. Actually, Mr. Kamath also speculated on the issue of unitary versus federal nature of the Constitution through a nice metaphor from physics. He said, “We cannot always guarantee that the unifying forces in the country – the centripetal forces – will gain ground against the centrifugal or the disruptive forces in our land.”[5]

To deal with this issue, his suggestion was that if the Lok Sabha voted for an amendment to the Constitution, which the Rajya Sabha rejected, it should be voted for again in the Lok Sabha – this time along with reasons for rejection by the Rajya Sabha. If the resolution wins votes the second time as well, it should be accepted.

Ira: Hold on. Isn’t that problematic? It is as if even though the Rajya Sabha is standing with federalism, the Lok Sabha would have the power to insist on an amendment only because they are a majority. To me, this seems like the Constitution would veer to the unitary side.

Anubhuti: You are right, which is why some people say we are a federal constitution regulated by articles 245 to 255, which allow the centre to exercise control on laws and policies of state governments.

The infamous 42nd amendment made during the emergency – which gives the legislature significantly more power than even the judiciary – had to be ratified by the Rajya Sabha and the President.

Ira: Wow! That is disturbing – closest we seem to have come to an armed revolution. By the way, what is the role of the president in all this? We do learn that the president is the chief of the State. Wouldn’t the president be able to refuse an amendment?

Anubhuti: That is a tricky question. The role of the president (and governors) is not political per se. He/she is meant to be safeguarding the Constitution, but under the advice of the legislature. The role is more administrative than political.

The judiciary – Supreme Court – has the power to reject any amendment to the Constitution.

Ira: What could the grounds for rejection be? Like Mr. Kamath said, what if at some point both the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha vote for changes to the Constitution that makes it unitary or less federal? I can imagine removal of processes and some powers from the states.

Anubhuti: There is something called the Basic Structure Doctrine in constitutional law, which the judiciary has the power to use. According to this doctrine, the legislature cannot change or destroy some fundamental features of the Constitution. These features would vary according to the Constitution. Remember, in the case for reservation for economically weaker sections, two judges argued that it violated the basic structure of the Constitution. Of course, three other judges on the bench said it did not.[6]

The basic structure could be interpreted differently in situations like this, but in simple terms it emphasizes that no amendment can go against the supremacy of the Constitution, the republican and democratic forms of government, secularism, separation of powers, federalism, and the rule of law.

We do know what republican and democratic forms mean – that all citizens participate directly or indirectly in policies and other governmental decisions – which takes care of fundamental rights and duties, and the directive principles of state policy.

The clause about the supremacy of the Constitution is interesting. This doctrine avers that we will remain a constitutional democracy – controlled by the Constitution, not the legislature or the executive. In principle, it ensures that even the government elected by a majority cannot say their party’s ideology is superior to the democratic principles defined in the Constitution.

Ira: You mean the social revolution that Mr. Kamath was worried about cannot happen in India?

Anubhuti: Yes and no. You see, somehow by exerting power, the central government did do this during the emergency in 1975. But it was revoked soon after the next elections in 1977, when another party came to power.

Ira: Aah! So, there was no choice about going for elections, in which all of us firmly rejected the party. You would say republican democracy prevailed!

Anubhuti: Yes, it did. We keep learning from our collective experiences with democratic processes; and as I always like to say, democracy is constantly a work in progress.

Ira: I knew you would say this! Also like you said, the Constitution is a palimpsest. Every Supreme Court judgment that interprets articles of the Constitution, overlays it with new meaning; and we learn how to be more democratic than we already are.

[1]https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/26-nov-1949/#130353

[2]https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/17-sep-1949/#123070

[3]https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/17-sep-1949/#123070

[4]https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/17-sep-1949/#123115

[5]https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/17-sep-1949/#123133

[6]https://www.scobserver.in/cases/janhit-abhiyan-union-of-india-ews-reservation-case-background/

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
Ira and Anubhuti discuss how India’s Constitution was built to allow for change. Although it is a final document, it is flexible, showcasing how democracy remains a work in progress.

item.title: Tackling student misbehaviour
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Tenzing Rapgyal

We are a school in the peaceful foothills of the Himalayas, but the year 2023 was anything but peaceful for us. The students of the 12th standard that year became notorious for being the most difficult batch to deal with. The school environment was also filled with negativity caused by a series of unfortunate incidents:

  • An acrimonious altercation between some senior students and members of the advisory committee on their hostel rounds during an evening self-study hour.
  • The public display of conflict between a teacher and a student during the morning assembly.
  • A theft in the school tuck shop by middle school students.
  • A viral case of some 10th class students caught on camera in an inebriated condition in their hostel.
  • An account of a student with a violent past running after the foster mother in the hostel.

What are the causes of such misbehaviour?

Corporal punishment was banned in our school in the early 2000s, making disciplining students a challenge for the teachers. Children, therefore, are free from the fear of punishments. Another challenge for the teachers is to make their classes innovative and interesting even as they race to finish the syllabus and get students exam-ready. While students are required to write internal assessments as well, they add only 20% to a student’s final result. It is the year-end final exam that makes up 80% of student assessment. This means that passing the final exam is much easier. All that a student has to do is to study intensively for a month or two before the final exam. Such a system seems to have been designed to help students pass, so that they don’t drop out of school. So, unless pushed by parents at home, students have neither intrinsic nor extrinsic motivation to study, leading to disciplinary problems in class and hostels. Trying to change this systemic failure is like hitting one’s head against a wall. But, that does not mean one should give up and surrender to reality. By being wise and realistic, one can deal with the situation, which is what I have been trying to do after falling prey to hopelessness myself.

What are the types of disciplinary problems in my class?

The common disciplinary hindrances that I have had to deal with are bunking, being late after break, sleeping even during the first period, irregular class work, incomplete home assignments, talking in class, not bringing the textbook, or being disruptive. Among these, it is being late to class that irritates me the most, because it disrupts the flow of the lesson. To deal with this nuisance, I have tried using humour. For instance, when students would knock on the classroom door while the lesson was going on, I would stop teaching and let them enter to a thunderous applause of the students already in the class. But, the punctual students were losing precious time because of this. Therefore, I made a rule that if students are late, but find the classroom door ajar, they should enter silently without asking for permission. If, on the other hand, the door is bolted from inside, the students will be considered absent, as they have been late for more than 20 minutes of the period.

What remedial measures I have taken

Over the last five years, I have devised a system of Six Levels of Remedial Measures. Earlier, my remedial steps were reactive because I did not have a system to deal with repeated lapses. There was no clarity about the consequences of a breach. As a result, often, there were verbal altercations between the problematic students and me. To avoid such confrontations, I came up with the following system, and so far the results have been satisfactory.

How effective have the remedial measures been?

To answer the question, let me present a quantified response. Since the beginning of the session in March 2023 to the end of January 2024, I had been keeping a record of the lapses on the part of the students, which number to 1049.

Tabulation of the number of cases

Graphical representation of the data

It is evident from the data that there was a steady decline in the number of cases across the different categories of lapses.

For example, the number of cases of being late/absent in Level 1 dropped from 93 to only one by Level 5. Sleeping students went down from 35 in Level 1 to three in Level 3, after which all students rectified this error. In the case of inattentiveness, out of 16 students in Level 1, only two crossed into Level 2. Regarding the regularity of students in their daily class work, 64 cases were recorded in Level 1, which decreased to merely one in Level 3.

Only in the case of ‘being late/absent’, seven students reached Level 4, which means that their parents/guardians were contacted to report the matter and requested to guide their wards and only one student was reported to the principal’s office for crossing over to Level 5. In the rest of the cases, after Level 2, which involves detention, there was a drastic decline with only three students being reported for sleeping to the headmaster’s office for crossing Level 2 and one student for his/her incomplete class work.

Were my remedial measures effective?

I think so. However, the cause for concern was the alarming number of cases of ‘being late/absent’ in Level 1, which numbered to 93 students out of 171 students of classes XI and XII. In this category, the decline in the case is almost half at each level till Level 4. What the data suggests is that again almost half of the total number of students took undue advantage of the three chances/warnings given in Level 1. Most of the cases in this category are students being late to class after the short recess and lunch break. In other categories of lapses, the breach in Level 1 is higher compared to the subsequent levels. My intention behind giving students three chances/warnings is to provide them opportunities to self-discipline without extrinsic remedial measures. But on studying the data, the question is whether I should do away with the three chances of intrinsic remedy. One encouraging aspect the data reveals is that students restrain from crossing Level 3, which can be deduced as fear or respect for their parents, which deter them from being reported.

Finally, what I have learned from implementing this system for three years is that if there are clearly defined consequences for a breach of classroom decorum, students regulate their behaviour. Some students may misuse the remedial opportunities given to them, but knowing their stories (of broken families, dysfunctional parents, death of a parent, and so on), I realize that I have to be sensitive to minor lapses when their internal self is in turmoil.

Tenzing Rapgyal is P.G.T. English. He works at Tibetan Children’s Village School in Dharamsala, Himachal Pradesh. He can be reached at tenrap83@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Tenzing Rapgyal
What does it take to turn chaos into calm? Tenzing Rapgyal shares a data-driven approach to ensure discipline in the classroom.

item.title: Under the tamarind grove
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat

After a short interval, the Backyard Discovery Group was becoming active again. Let us see what places the group members are going to explore this time.

I found a message from Sujatha in our Backyard Discovery WhatsApp group. “Nandini, we had a good experience at the university. Our children’s annual exams will be over by the end of March. Can you suggest a place to visit before we leave for our hometown?”

“I will think about it and get back to you. In the meanwhile, if someone else also knows, please do suggest,” I replied.

I started thinking about places around the city where there wouldn’t be much of a crowd, and where we could leisurely spend time and learn something.

The next evening, Sujatha and Madhavi came to my house. They started discussing their children’s exams and our activities. Suddenly, Sujatha pointed to the bookshelf and asked, “Nandini, what is that wooden thing on the bookshelf?”

I took the wooden box out and opened it in front of them. “This is Halaguli mane. It is a traditional board game. Two people can play the game. We should count and fill each cup with an equal number of tamarind seeds on both sides. The game starts with removing the seeds from one cup and distributing them in successive cups,” I explained.

Madhavi added, “We call this Pallanguzhi. It is simple but tricky at the same time. We can play many different kinds of games with this. Children can also learn counting. We played with seashells when we were young, as we lived in a coastal town.”

“Interesting. Now, where can we get so many seeds? We use tamarind paste for cooking these days,” Sujatha lamented.

“We can go to the Nallur Tamarind Grove and collect seeds,” I replied with a smile.

Sujatha and Madhavi said together, “What …tamarind grove? Where is it?”

“It is on the outskirts of the city. We can go for a picnic after the children's exams,” I said.

“Oh! I have never heard about it. I will book a vehicle. We can start early in the morning and spend the whole day,” Madhavi said enthusiastically.

The picnic day was fixed and everyone exchanged messages on what to carry, where to start from, and how to reach. We started early to enjoy the morning breeze in the hot summer. As we moved out of the city, we observed rose gardens on both sides of the road.

Vivan asked, “How come so many rose gardens here? All different colours of roses are growing.”

“Oh! I never expected so many huge gardens. Look here, you can see vineyards too,” Shilpa pointed.

“Yes dear, all these crops are cultivated on a large scale. They supply to the city market and also export. Unless we come out of the city once in a while, we will miss these beautiful sights,” I said.

We came upon a signboard with an arrow mark that said Hunasethopu. The driver turned the vehicle in that direction.

“Is that a signboard for the Tamarind Grove?” Rini asked.

“Yes, tamarind is called hunase in Kannada, imli in Hindi, and chintapandu in Telugu,” the driver said.

As we reached the location, the driver parked the vehicle in front of a temple, which was dedicated to the local goddess, Gangamma. Everyone stepped out and gathered near the temple. “Let's walk around this temple before the weather gets too hot. Carry your water bottles and wear your caps,” I instructed.

“It is amazing. Here, all the trees are only tamarind trees. I have never seen such a huge area full of tamarind trees,” Madhavi said.

“Come here, read this information board to get some idea about this place,” I moved towards the board.

Anand started reading loudly, “This grove is in Nallur village, near Devanahalli. The tamarind grove is around 250 acres. It is believed that tamarind trees originated here in the 13th century when the Chola kings ruled this area. From that time, it is considered a sacred grove. In the grove, approximately 300 trees can be found. One tree is around 410 years old. It has been identified through carbon dating. Others may be more than 200 years old. The Government of Karnataka declared this a heritage site under the Biodiversity Act 2002.”

“I want to see the oldest tree. Let's run,” Vivan shouted and started running. The other children followed.

“These children do not pay attention to anything; in which direction are they running?” Sujatha asked in frustration.

“Not to worry, they will come back, then we will all go together,” I said.

As the children returned, breathing heavily, I said, “If you want to see this grove, you must follow me. Walk carefully, the Lantana plants growing on the sides may irritate your skin.”

“Do we find snakes here?” Rini asked.

“Of course, you will find many small animals like mongoose, hare, snake, and chameleon. Observe the different kinds of birds and butterflies,” I said.

We moved along a narrow walkway. We observed many old and young trees. The children jumped and plucked tamarind fruits from low-hanging branches.

“My mouth is watering just by looking at these fruits. It is tasty to eat unripe fruit with salt.” Madhavi said.

“When we just think of gooseberries or tamarind, why does our mouth start to water?” Shilpa asked.

“Because you've already tasted it. The smell, taste, or even the sight of some foods releases saliva to digest it,” I said.

“Look at the canopy, it is spread like a big umbrella. See these pink flowers. They are beautiful. I never saw tamarind flowers before,” Madhavi said.

“The other tree has very few branches and leaves. Each tree is different,” Shilpa pointed.

I called everyone. “All of you come here. This tree is very huge. The bark is gnarled. It is hollow from the centre. But it has green foliage. This is one of the oldest trees.”

“It looks so strange. Why is it so knobbly?” Shilpa asked.

“Over the period, it must have experienced many insect infestations and abrasions due to wind, rain, or human acts. But it recovered from the damage and kept growing. That is why it is twisted,” I replied.

“I wonder how only tamarind trees are here,” Anand said.

“It is a multipurpose tree. It grows well in less rainfed areas, without much maintenance. People who lived here must have planted a few, and later they multiplied,” I said.

“Look here what I got! We can play with this today,” Anand said, showing the seeds he had collected.

“There is an old temple here. Let us sit there for some time and then leave,” Madhavi called everyone. All of us sat on the stone bench to relax.

Suggested activities

  1. Make a list of food items that one adds tamarind to.
  2. Which other sour fruits are used in cooking?
  3. Name the different types of seeds that are used for playing games.

References

http://nbaindia.org/content/106/29/1/bhs.html

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamarind

https://citizenmatters.in/biodiversity-heritage-site-nallur-tamarind-grove-devanahalli-30-jan-2016/

Nandini Dholepat is a botanist who has been involved in teaching and research for more than 25 years. Currently, she is engaged as a consultant for Intellectual Property Rights for Karnataka State Council for Science and Technology, Bengaluru, and is a visiting faculty member at RIWATCH, Roing, Arunachal Pradesh. She can be reached at nandinidholepat@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat
A picnic to collect tamarind seeds turned into a lesson in history and ecology. Nandini Dholepat’s piece reiterates connecting with the natural world.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: A visit to Nallur Tamarind Grove at Devanahalli

item.title: Thinking about thinking
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

How does learning happen? We may say it happens through discovery, exploration, experimentation, through exposure to information or experience. There are several ways. However, at the core of it, only when we think and reflect about it does learning happen. Learning is a consequence of thinking. This brings me to the fascinating topic of thinking about thinking, which we almost never think about!

Where does thinking happen? Why is thinking important? Do we think all the time? Do we all think in the same way? Are there different ways of thinking? In what language do we think? Can we think without a language? Do creatures other than humans think? If they do, how do they think? Can we improve the way we think?
