I’m going to borrow parts of a brilliant book, Philosophy for Children, written by philosopher and writer, Professor Sundar Sarukkai, which has a chapter on ‘thinking’ that beautifully breaks down this process for us.

Let’s begin with the most basic question that Sarukkai asks – what do you do when you think? If you have to explain what you do when you are eating, it’s easy. You take some food, put it in your mouth, then bite and chew, and finally swallow it. Similarly, you can explain step-by-step what you do when you run, jump, cycle, etc.

Take a moment and try to describe what it is that you do when you think!

It’s difficult, isn’t it! Sarukkai encourages the reader to note down what happens to your body when you are thinking. He asks if there is a place in your body where you do your thinking. We may have been taught that we think in our mind or brain, but can you feel your brain thinking just like you can feel that your mouth and throat are cold when you are eating ice-cream? Is it possible that we can also think in our heart? Or in our stomach – after all it does rumble when we are hungry and act funny just before the math exam! Sarukkai provokes the reader further by asking several questions like – when you run, your body sweats; when you think, does your body sweat? Do you feel tired? What part of your body becomes active when you are thinking? We can say that thinking is talking or arguing with yourself. But for a cricketer, it’s the body that thinks when a ball is coming their way – they have no time to talk to themselves in that moment!

There are endless ways humans think and the better we know how we think, the better we learn. This curiosity to understand HOW we think has led several educators and researchers to design tools to make thinking visible. Project Zero by Harvard University is one such initiative where they designed several Thinking Routines to create cultures of thinking within schools.

A social studies teacher in class 2 teaching a unit on ‘types of houses’ recently shared this image in her class (please add the AI image) and used a popular thinking routine called See-Think-Wonder. She first asked the children to state ‘What it is they SEE’ in the image. Students threw up answers like:

‘a city with skyscrapers and jhopdis’

‘an old city and a new city’

‘different kinds of houses and buildings’

‘brick houses with roofs and tall glass buildings’

‘colourful houses and blue and grey buildings’

This was a great entry point into the topic. As the image reflected a stark difference in the types of houses, she then asked the children ‘Why do you THINK there are such different kinds of houses in a city?’ The responses of students as young as these were absolutely intriguing.

‘…because rich people live in tall buildings and poor people live in the small ones.’

Teacher: “What makes you say that?”

‘…because tall buildings are fancy and expensive. It doesn’t take a lot of money to make small houses like in the picture.’

Another response was:

‘…we need different types of houses now because tall buildings have more flats.’

Teacher: “…and why do we need more flats? What makes you say that?”

‘…because there are so many more people living in the city than before.’

Another child added in:

‘…yes, my mother always says that when she was my age there was not so much traffic, so many cars, so many people in Mumbai.’

The teacher layered the conversation with an additional thinking routine ‘What makes you say that?’ allowing the learners to make the thoughts behind their thinking visible. The discussion ranged from how the city was changing to socio-economic realities, also making room for personal experiences to come in. Many students talked about re-development of their own building – a dinner-table conversation in every home in a city like Mumbai.

The teacher decided to end the class by giving the third part of the thinking routine as homework. She asked the learners to write a list of questions starting with: “I WONDER who/why/what/which/where/how…” using any of these question starters. This allowed absolute freedom of one’s curiosity to know beyond what is obvious and students were so excited to do this homework, many of them began right away even before the class was over.

Of course, the teacher would find her way to stilt houses, igloos, and mud huts and all the other things mentioned in the syllabus, but to use this opportunity to get learners thinking ensured they were invested on this learning journey. There are several such thinking routines (think-pair-share, think-puzzle-explore, circle of viewpoints–a resource link is provided at the end of the article), each with their own objectives and provocations but all with the single aim to encourage students to be curious and for them to see and know what and how they are thinking about things.

Unfortunately, we don’t draw much attention to the quality of questions we ask in the class. The questions given at the end of a textbook chapter are usually recall-based, closed questions and a few reasoning questions, which the learner is expected to answer with reference to the given text. None of these encourage you to think. And to top it all is our new friend AI, which almost encourages you not to think!

Over the last few years, I have been encouraging teachers to identify the ‘nature’ of the questions before crafting them in order to make conscious efforts to bring about deeper thinking. It requires a little bit of extra work, in an already very busy life of a teacher, to create a set of ‘thinking questions’ for every lesson plan. Take a look at the shift in the nature of questions in a grade 5 and a nursery class and you’ll get an idea of the impact it can have on thinking. The quality of learning in the class improved dramatically as teachers became conscious of the kinds of questions students were asking. After the initial rigour of consciously wading through this new competency that was thrown at them, it soon became an effortless way of being. They couldn’t ask a closed, recall-based question without noticing it anymore!

Bringing about cultures of thinking in schools is the need of the hour and it is essential that teachers themselves internalize this culture first. As part of a programme I co-design, we introduced Socratic Seminars with teachers so they can experience how exciting and enriching a collaborative thinking process can be. A Socratic Seminar is a structured discussion method, where participants collaboratively explore a piece of text through thoughtful questioning and dialogue. It's not a debate, but rather a process of collective meaning-making where participants analyze, interpret, and build upon each other's ideas.

A teacher shared this after their first Socratic Seminar experience: “For the very first time ever I participated and engaged in a Socratic Seminar. It gave me an opportunity to understand and learn how to analyze complex texts and questions…. fostering my own critical thinking and to engage in meaningful dialogue…..which in turn prompted me on my own questioning techniques.” Another teacher said: “When we get into discussions, we are always arguing, agreeing, and disagreeing on viewpoints. This was the first time I felt all of us were interested in figuring out together what the article was trying to say. The format of the seminar pushes you to be a better listener and build on other’s ideas instead of countering them. Made me think of discussions in a new light!”

Learning as a collective endeavour in an educational setting is ideal. It allows for participants, both adults and children, to actively engage in discussion, critique, and refinement of ideas and thought. If learning is a consequence of thinking, then perhaps we can begin to see thinking too as a collective enterprise. A tiny big shift in our classrooms – ready to give it a shot! What do you think?

References

  • Philosophy for Children, Sundar Sarukkai, published by Ektara
  • Project Zero – Harvard University, https://pz.harvard.edu/thinking-routines

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator, and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD in teacher education. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Can we feel our brain the way we feel ice-cream? Timira invites you to think about thinking itself.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: thanks for writing and sharing a good article.

item.title: Oplus_131072
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.excerpt:encoded: Oplus_131072

item.title: Is craft-centred education the way to go?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nimesh Ved

This book is an outcome of the author, Nidhi Gaur’s, study of Anand Niketan in Wardha, Maharashtra. This is a school where “Work is not associated with particular people; rather, it is perceived simply as work that needs to be done.” The first chapter, Introduction, deftly conveys what the book is all about; it states, “So far, craft-centred education has not been studied from the perspective of gender, so this study revisits an older idea with a new perspective.” The book has been neatly structured over nine chapters spread across 181 pages. The final chapter, Implications and conclusion, succinctly sums up the learning – “The examination of the pedagogy of crafts has shown the potential of craft-centred education in the holistic development of the self of the students.”

The author “examines the interrelation of gender and the pedagogy of crafts” in depth. She looks at the history and trajectory of crafts and gender juxtaposed with education. She is immersed in the topic and gleans out knowledge from the books she referred to and the children (at Anand Niketan) she interacted with. These she deftly puts forth in the book. She also pauses to clarify terms and concepts (such as modernity and modernization) where the text warrants. She talks about “rural” India, but is aware of the irony that today “the village is referred to as a thing of the past, far away from the ‘active’ space”. The oeuvre of references is rich as well; she brings in John Galsworthy’s classic short story, Quality, for example, to highlight a point on craftsmanship. She underscores that “craft-centred education has the potential to create a culture of patience, reflective action, expression, coexistence and peace” in a manner befitting the topic.

The book challenges the existing paradigm of school education. In telling us what a craft-centred education can achieve, it points to what we are missing in our conventional schools today. When, for example, the author says of Anand Niketan, “The school was also found to be emerging as a space where students could express and experiment with their gendered roles to understand other people’s responses and behaviours towards their experimentation,” we go back to the schools we have been associated with as students or teachers and wonder how we had explored gender and what could have been done differently. When a child tells her, “We are taught to study while we learn craft by doing it with our hands,” or when she observes that, “The practice of crafts is based on the methods of science,” we pause and absorb. This is where the book scores; it nudges us to think. The book also has lessons for today – it informs us that there were critiques on a craft-centred education policy, a little less than a century ago; in other words, education policy matters used to be read and discussed! It reminds us that to move forward as a society, we need to get education into the public consciousness and discourses.

The editing could have been tighter. Some things are repeated (e.g.: Devi Prasad’s introduction and work) and there are errors too (e.g.: it is Zakir Hussain on one page (44) and Zakir Husain on the next (45)). Cliches like “her ngo” too could have been avoided. Could it also have looked at Gandhi with a more nuanced and balanced lens?

The book, however, like all good books, left me with questions:

  • Why talk about this school at all when it has neither flourished nor its model replicated?
  • Why discuss craft-centred education with an English reading-writing audience; an audience whose aesthetics are not exactly thriving?
  • What motivates the teachers in this school? How were their roles envisaged in the initial design?
  • Teacher capacity has been a bottleneck in conventional schools; how different is it here?
  • Are there other schools, today, that adhere to a craft-centred curriculum? Is a craft-centred curriculum relevant only to the rural areas?
  • How is craft education being imparted in our schools today? Has it become restricted to the privileged (read fancy) schools?
  • Finally, what if we replace crafts with sports? How would it impact the attributes the author ascribes to craft-based education – gender equity, working with hands, quality, balance, initiative, interdependence?

Nimesh enjoys being amidst books and children. He blogs at https://nimesh-ved.blogspot.com/ and can be reached at nimesh.explore@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nimesh Ved
What if school was built around craft, not just textbooks? Nimesh Ved’s book review presents how craft-centred education might help children think and learn better.
